The velocity dispersion profile of Draco has previously been fitted by using a single stellar component with a radially varying orbital anisotropy embedded in a massive halo, and imply a similar anisotropy profile is required for Leo I if it resides in an NFW halo.

At this position, the line-of sight velocity distribution would be best characterized by a double Maxwellian distribution, distinguishing it from other proposals such as a radially-varying orbital anisotropy (see for application of this idea to Draco). The ratio of central-to-maximum velocity dispersion provides a lower bound to the ratio of the central velocity dispersions of the two components. Interpreting Leo I as a two-component King model, the data shown in Figure fig:ldf suggest that the central component has a central (projected) velocity dispersion of 6 - 7 km s ^ 1 , and that the outer component has _ p_2 (0) 13 14 km s ^ 1 . If Leo I is a multiple component system, we expect that the line-of-sight velocity distribution of stars at about R 150-200 pc should reveal evidence for the double Maxwellian distribution discussed previously. Similar testable inferences can be made for Fornax and Draco.

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as one of six bright dwarf spheroidals, notes profile and stellar mass assumptions, and ranks it with Ursa Minor among the best annihilation targets.

quotes.quote: We show that Ursa Minor and Draco are the most promising dSphs for ray detection with GLAST and other planned observatories.

We note that for the particular case of Draco, recent studies have used a Plummer instead of a King profile, as described in . Using a Plummer profile has no effect to this calculation, because the primary difference in the fits is in the outer regions of Draco where the surface density is exponentially declining. In order to estimate the total mass in stars and its contribution to the total gravitational potential, we need to determine the typical range of stellar mass-to-light ratios for dSphs. Draco was considered in Lokas:2004sw , where they quote an upper limit to the stellar mass-to-light ratio of 3 , implying a total stellar mass of 6.6 10^5 .
Assuming a smooth dark matter distribution in the dSph halos, we find that Ursa Minor and Draco are the most promising dSph's for detecting products of dark matter annihilation.

sources.summary: The paper finds Draco has an uncomfortably high MOND mass-to-light ratio, exceptional anisotropy behavior, and possible MOND tidal disruption despite luminosity and distance revisions.

quotes.quote: Sextans and Draco, however, have M/L= 9.2_ 3.0 ^ +5.3 and 43.9_ 19.3 ^ +29.0 respectively, which poses a problem. Apart from the need for Sextans' integrated magnitude to be reviewed, we propose tidal effects intrinsic to MOND, testable with numerical simulations, but fully orbit dependant, which are disrupting Draco.

Most dwarfs have very little sensitivity to the rate at which the anisotropy decreases with radius, with the only exception being Draco. There can be little doubt that Draco and now Sextans are both concerns for MOND.

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as a well-studied validation case; its maximum-likelihood structural parameters and half-light radius agree with previous SDSS and deeper-data measurements.

quotes.quote: Dra - Applying our ML fitting to the Draco galaxy provides a good opportunity to verify that the algorithm works properly on a galaxy that has been well studied. Our structural parameters are very similar to previous determinations by =88 3 and =0.29 0.02 odenkirchen01b , also from SDSS data. The half-light radius we measure is in excellent agreement with that obtained from deeper data by r_h=10.09 0.02' , although this value is derived from their best Plummer model segall07 . This comparison gives confidence in the results of the ML algorithm.

sources.summary: The paper compares Draco's low-metallicity MDF with the Galactic halo, notes CaT calibration effects and HES-selection corrections, and cites high-resolution evidence for very metal-poor Draco stars.

quotes.quote: The detailed abundance analyses of Cohen/Huang:2009 suggest that [ Fe/H ] [ Ca/H ] (CaT,Winnick) 0.2 for Draco giants; this yields the open circles in the Draco panel of Fig.  Fig:dSph . The (constant) offset arises primarily from the lower [ Ca/Fe ] seen among dSph giants as compared to GC giants, and includes any error in the calibration adopted by Winnick:2003 . The MDF for Draco, with 24 stars at [ Fe/H ] < -2.3 (nine of which have [ Ca/H ] < -2.3 ), and for UMi, agree reasonably well with the halo Fe-MDF, once the selection efficiency of the HES is taken into consideration.

Cohen/Huang:2009 have obtained high resolution spectra of a sample of stars in the Draco dSph, one of the more luminous of the dSph satellites of the Galaxy, and found one star with [ Fe/H ] < -3.0 in that dSph, in addition to a Draco giant at [ Fe/H ] =-2.97 discovered earlier by Shetroneetal:1998 .

sources.summary: VERITAS observed Draco as a Local Group dwarf galaxy dark-matter target, noting existing spectroscopic data and reporting no significant gamma-ray emission with roughly 1 percent Crab flux upper limits.

quotes.quote: VERITAS undertook observations of three dwarf galaxies during the period 2007-2008: Draco, Ursa Minor, Willman I. Draco and Ursa Minor are relatively high surface brightness objects for which large spectroscopic data sets exist. Willman I belongs to the group of faint stellar systems that were recently discovered in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey willman05 . Although Willman I shares some similarities with tidally disrupted globular clusters, the extreme mass-to-light ratio inferred from its stellar velocity dispersion martin07 suggests that it is a dwarf galaxy. strigari08 have projected that this system may have significantly larger DM annihilation signal than that calculated for previously known dwarf galaxies.

VERITAS took 20 hours of observation on both Draco and Ursa Minor from March 2007 to May 2007 and 15 hours of observation on Willman I from December 2007 to Feburary 2008. No significant ray emission were detected, and the 95 C.L. upper limits on the integral ray flux from each source were set at the level of approximately 1 percent of the Crab Nebula flux.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Chandra observations of Draco for decaying dark matter, finds its spectrum nearly matches blank sky background, and derives conservative sterile-neutrino constraints.

quotes.quote: We have searched for line emission from decaying dark matter particles in Chandra X-ray observations of the Milky Way dwarf galaxy Draco. The Draco and blank sky background spectra have a nearly identical shape. This confirms dwarfs as ideal for studying dark matter emission since the baryonic contamination is close to zero. No obvious line signal is found, which leads to conservative constraints in the m_s - ^2(2 ) parameter space for sterile neutrinos.

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as a dark-matter dominated bright dwarf spheroidal, notes it alone is consistent with hosts above 24 km/s, and contrasts its low luminosity with a comparatively massive halo.

quotes.quote: Our final sample therefore contains 9 dwarf spheroidals: Fornax, Leo I, Sculptor, Leo II, Sextans, Carina, Ursa Minor, Canes Venatici I, and Draco. All of these galaxies are known to be dark matter dominated at mateo1998 : wolf2010 find that their dynamical mass-to-light ratios at range from 10-300 .
It is immediately apparent that all of the bright dSphs are consistent with NFW subhalos of = 12-24 , and only one dwarf (Draco) is consistent with > 24 .
Ursa Minor and Draco, which are 40-80 times less luminous than Fornax, sit in halos that are comparable or slightly more massive, and therefore have inferred efficiencies of closer to =0.002 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as a low-stellar-mass dSph example whose early star-formation shutdown implies early Milky Way accretion and helps motivate baryonic satellite evolution.

quotes.quote: For instance, Draco and Ursa Minor stopped forming stars 10 Gyr ago Grebel:1998uz , providing evidence for early accretion on to the MW.

As an example, we examine Draco and Ursa Minor, which have M_* 3 10^5 McConnachie:2012fh , are at similar distances D_ MW 76 McConnachie:2012fh and are expected to inhabit haloes with 25 . Both show SF occurring at early times and shutting down 10 Gyr ago Grebel:1998uz , implying their accretion around redshift two. Therefore, the low stellar mass of objects such as Draco is a result of the shut-down of SF after early accretion on to the MW.

sources.summary: The review discusses Draco kinematic datasets and dynamical models, including flat dispersion profiles, extended dark halos, anisotropy, and NFW-like dark matter slopes.

quotes.quote: Even though the very first measurement of the line-of-sight velocity dispersion of a dSph was based on just 3 carbon stars in Draco aaronson1983 , it already hinted at a dynamical mass-to-light ratio about one order of magnitude larger than for globular clusters. The first modeling attempts already showed that mass following light models could not fit the relatively flat velocity dispersion profiles observed, and that extended dark matter halos were needed, for example in the case of Draco Kleyna2001 . In Kleyna2002draco they have applied this modeling to a dataset for Draco with 160 member stars, and found that the system is best fit by a slightly tangentially anisotropic ellipsoid and with a halo that falls off more slowly than a flat rotation curve model ( v_c ^ 0.17 ), while they are also able to rule out a mass-follows-light model and an extended harmonic core with 3 confidence.
They model Draco in this way, and find that the preferred model is a power-law, with a slope quite similar to the NFW, that is _ DM = 1 for 20 700 pc, and that the velocity ellipsoid is radial.

sources.summary: Draco is modeled as strongly influenced by the Milky Way external field; the predicted low mass-to-light ratios conflict with several velocity-dispersion estimates.

quotes.quote: Draco Draco is the faintest of the classical satellites, and it is located at a small Galactic distance and is thus strongly influenced by the gravitational potential of the MW. As a consequence, its internal dynamics are dictated by this external field, and the dynamical mass-to-light ratio (see Fig.  fig:draco ) is thus radius independent: _ dyn () _ b () , and M_ dyn /M_* 8.5 . Thus M_ dyn /L 8.5 M_*/L Draco The central shape of the PDM halo is thus cored, because the baryonic density profile is cored. Furthermore, the predicted M_ dyn /L are neither sensitive to the exact density model (see Section  sect:varyR ) nor to the observational uncertainty of Draco's total luminosity (see Section  sect:varyL ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as a local dwarf spheroidal target whose central black-hole scenario can dramatically strengthen dark-matter annihilation constraints, especially under the Tremaine black-hole mass relation.

quotes.quote: Notice that work along these same lines, prior to the Fermi LAT era, was presented by one of us and Collaborators in Ref. , for the specific case of Draco.

We note that for Draco and, especially, for Ursa Minor the effect of a central black hole is dramatic, and leads to constraints, for the particular b b annihilation pathway, of up to almost four orders of magnitude stronger than the classic pair-annihilation cross section value invoked for thermal decoupling production of DM in the early universe (for s-wave annihilation), 3 10^ 26 cm ^3 /s (we shade in grey a representative range for around that central value).

For example, when using the Tremaine relation the Draco and UMi galaxies are the most constraining, while for the Magorrian relation the more luminous Fornax galaxy is expected to host a comparatively much larger black hole, producing the most stringent limits. Notice that while in both cases Fornax is the galaxy that hosts the most massive BH, it is only when we use the Magorrian relation that the effect of the BH dominates over the fact that UMi and Draco are significantly closer than Fornax.

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as a classical dwarf with Hectochelle kinematics and finds it has one of the largest expected annihilation and decay fluxes among the classical dwarfs.

quotes.quote: For Draco, Leo II, and Ursa Minor we use stellar-kinematic data acquired with the Hectochelle spectrograph at the 6.5-m MMT. These data have previously been analyzed by 2009ApJ...704.1274W and 2011MNRAS.418.1526C , and will soon be made public (Walker, Olszewski Mateo, in preparation).
Concerning the overall amplitude of an annihilation signal, we find that among the classical dwarfs Draco and Ursa Minor have the largest expected flux.
In the case of decay, the kinematic analysis shows that Draco, and to a lesser extent, Sextans and Sculptor are the sources with the largest expected emission.

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as a dwarf spheroidal dark-matter comparison target, adopting its J factor and solid angle and comparing projected annihilation flux with CTA and Fermi sensitivities.

quotes.quote: For comparison, we also consider DM annihilation in the Draco dwarf spheroidal galaxy, which, to a good approximation, is a point source to both CTA and Fermi at low energies (in the upper parts of their respective energy ranges, both would observe Draco as a somewhat extended source). For this analysis, we use the J factor and solid angle from Table 1 of Ref. \ : _ Draco = 2.4 10^ 4 sr, J_ Draco = 6.31 10^ 18 GeV ^2 cm ^ 5 .

For comparison, we show the signal flux expected from the dwarf spheroidal galaxy Draco, in the case of a DM particle with m_ =1 TeV mass, annihilating into b b final states with an annihilation cross section of v = 3 10^ 26 cm^3 s^ 1 . Draco is one of the most promising targets, and the envelope shows the uncertainty in the projected signal flux, which is primarily related to its overall mass (taken from Ref. ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Draco as a robust, potentially bright classical dwarf-spheroidal target, using updated kinematic data and finding larger J factors under flexible modeling.

quotes.quote: For Draco, we adopt the data set of 2015arXiv150302589W , which includes measurements for 500 members.

The most striking example is the case of Draco. The Fermi collaboration 2014PhRvD..89d2001A,2015arXiv150302641F reported the ray observations of 25 dSph galaxies and conducted a stacking analysis of 15 of them to set constraints on the DM annihilation cross section.
At the optimal angle _ ^ J 2r_h /d , the `classical' dSphs UMi and Draco are confirmed as the potentially-brightest and most favoured targets in terms of J factors.

sources.summary: The paper compares two Draco proper-motion measurements and finds consistent rotation-amplitude estimates and similar kinematic position angles, adopting the HST value for its dwarf-galaxy analysis.

quotes.quote: Draco: Two recent proper motion measurements for Draco differ by several standard deviations: ( 17.7 6.3,-22.1 6.3 ) Pryor2015 and ( 28.4 ± 4.7, -28.9 4.1 ) Casetti-Dinescu2016 . Casetti-Dinescu2016 discuss possible reasons for the discrepancy, but are unable to determine one. We run our analysis with both measurements, which lead to values of 2.62_ 0.90 ^ +0.86 , and =0.29_ 0.10 ^ +0.10 respectively. The two measurements of are within one sigma of one another and have roughly the same kinematic position angle ( 101 versus 105 ).

sources.summary: Deep XMM-Newton observations of the Draco dwarf spheroidal found no 3.5 keV line, strongly disfavoring a dark-matter decay origin for that signal.

quotes.quote: Profumo Tesla Jeltema ^ 1 tesla@ucsc.edu and Stefano Profumo ^ 1 profumo@ucsc.edu ^ 1 Department of Physics and Santa Cruz Institute for Particle Physics University of California, Santa Cruz, CA 95064, USA We searched for an X-ray line at energies around 3.5 keV in deep, 1.6 Msec XMM-Newton observations of the dwarf spheroidal galaxy Draco. No line was found in either the MOS or the PN detectors. The data in this energy range are completely consistent with a single, unfolded power law modeling the particle background, which dominates at these energies, plus instrumental lines; the addition of a 3.5 keV line feature gives no improvement to the fit. The corresponding upper limit on the line flux rules out a dark matter decay origin for the 3.5 keV line found in observations of clusters of galaxies and in the Galactic Center at greater than 99 C.L..

sources.summary: The paper finds Draco has the largest classical-dSph J factor and the strongest D-factor prospects, making it a robust and detectable dark-matter decay target.

quotes.quote: Secondly, we find that among analysed dSphs, the ultra-faint dwarf galaxies Triangulum II and Ursa Major II are the most promising but large uncertain targets for dark matter annihilation while the classical dSph Draco is the most robust and detectable target for dark matter decay.

It is found from Table table3 and this figure that among the classical dSphs, Draco has the largest J factor value, while Fornax and Sculptor have the smallest uncertainties of J owing to an abundance of kinematic data.

In contrast to annihilation case, the classical dSphs have systematically higher D values than the UFDs, and the galaxy that has the highest D values is not a UFD galaxy but the classical dSph Draco. Moreover, Draco has smaller uncertainties of D factor than the other UFD galaxies and, thus, would be the most promising target among the Milky Way satellites.

sources.summary: The MAGIC review describes Draco as an early dwarf-spheroidal dark-matter target, selected for its high mass-to-light ratio and well-measured stellar motions, with about 8 hours observed.

quotes.quote: One can easily see that the first MAGIC DM observations, with the single-telescope, were devoted mostly to short (less than 30h) observation of DSGs: Draco, Willman 1 and Segue 1. Draco was considered one of the best candidate at that time, because of its large mass-to-light ratio, and precise estimation of the stellar motion thanks to a sample of thousands of star members. It was observed for about 8h. In 2007, the Sloan Digital Sky Survey started to produce high-precision photometric data on a newly discovered class of DSG, the so-called ``ultra-faint'', due to the fact that the number of member stars were a factor of 10 less than the previously discovered ``classical'' DSG (including Draco).

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as the benchmark dwarf target for sub-GeV dark matter photon searches, noting its angular size and finding Draco searches more sensitive than diffuse-emission searches, especially for annihilation.

quotes.quote: Following , we consider constraints arising from diffuse emission in the Galactic halo, and from emission from one particular dwarf (Draco).
The smallest target we will consider here is Draco, with an angular size of 1.3^ , and we assume that the angular resolution is smaller than this size.

In the case of either dark matter decay or dark matter annihilation, the sensitivity arising from a search for emission from Draco exceeds the sensitivity of a search for diffuse emission. But this improvement is more pronounced for the case of dark matter annihilation, as in this case the photon signal scales as the square of the dark matter density in the dwarf.

sources.summary: The paper presents Draco as a dense Milky Way satellite whose close pericenter passage could have enhanced its central dark-matter density in SIDM models.

quotes.quote: Such a scenario could explain the dense and compact cores of dwarf galaxies in the Local Group like Tucana (isolated from the Milky Way), the classical Milky Way satellite Draco, and some of the ultrafaint satellites.

Similar to Tucana, the MW satellite Draco (dwarf spheroidal) has a large central density , which may be difficult to explain with cross sections _m > 1 ^2/ , assuming evolution like a field halo . Draco most likely had a close pericenter passage according to Gaia data and hence our arguments would suggest it could also have enhanced its central density compared to the field SIDM halo evolution.
The same arguments suggest that the central dark matter density of satellites that have come close to the MW disk, like Draco and the ultrafaint dwarfs, would be enhanced for large cross sections.

sources.summary: The paper models Draco with axisymmetric Jeans equations and finds a strongly elongated dark matter halo can match its observed velocity-dispersion profiles.

quotes.quote: More precisely, we solve axisymmetric Jeans equations and apply them to the kinematic data of the eight classical dSphs: Draco, Ursa Minor, Carina, Sextans, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor and Fornax in order to investigate the global shape of ULA dark halo as well as to constrain on particle mass of ULA dark matter.

The Galactic Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy Data In this work, we adopt the photometric and spectroscopic data of the resolved member stars in the eight luminous dSphs in the Milky Way: Draco, Ursa Minor, Carina, Sextans, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor, and Fornax. This is because each have 200 stars with line-of-sight velocities from spectroscopy and well-constrained stellar distributions from photometry, so that these sample volumes might be enough to obtain more stringent limits on the mass range of m_ than those from other fainter dSphs such as ultra faint dwarf galaxies.

However, in combination with the effects of a tangentially-biased velocity anisotropy ( _z<0 ) and a large core radius of ULA dark matter halo ( r_c/b_ >1 ), the shapes of dispersion profiles computed by a strongly elongated dark matter halo are in good agreement with the observed profiles especially for Draco.

sources.summary: The paper describes Draco as a compact, dense Milky Way satellite whose properties are naturally explained in SIDM by formation in a high-concentration, core-collapsed subhalo.

quotes.quote: The overall effect for /m 3 \ cm^2/g is to increase the range of inner densities, potentially explaining the observed diversity of Milky Way satellites, which include compact systems like Draco and Segue 1 that are dense in dark matter, and less dense, diffuse systems like Sextans and Crater II.

Compare and contrast the cases of Crater II and Draco -- they both have similar pericenter distances , similar luminosities (within a factor of 2) but they have very different half-light radii and very different DM densities . This has a natural explanation in SIDM models in terms of Draco forming in a high-concentration halo and Crater II in a low concentration halo. Compact ultra-faint dSphs like Segue 1 would also form in high-concentration subhalos like Draco, but the subhalos would likely have lower masses similar to what we have simulated.

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco to illustrate low-mass dark-matter bound degradation when background profiling lowers the high-energy background and compensates with light annihilation signal.

quotes.quote: We note the possibility of a non-trivial bound degradation at low DM mass associated to our hypothesis of full correlation across energy bins, when profiling over both J and b , taking the case of Draco as an example. This can arise when the background matches well the counts at low energy, but overestimates the counts at high energy. In the particular situation of Draco, the optimisation algorithm prefers to lower the overall level of background in order to fit better the high energy part and, in order to compensate for the newly-created deficit at low energy, to add some light DM annihilation contribution (which does not contribute to the counts at the highest energies).
The degradation of the combined bound at high mass follows from the previously mentioned background overestimate at high energy in the case of Draco.

sources.summary: The paper models Draco in MOND, placing it among dwarfs where the external field is highly relevant and where high mass-to-light ratios yield a substantial phantom-dark-matter offset.

quotes.quote: Figure InternalFieldCompare clearly shows that for the dwarfs Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextans, and Carina, which are located in the top left corner for low stellar mass-to-light ratios, the EFE is expected to be substantially more relevant than for the dwarfs Leo I and Leo II, located in the bottom right corner for high stellar mass-to-light ratios.
When the external field is much larger than the internal field (Carina, Draco, Sextans, and Ursa Minor), MOND predicts a Newtonian-like behaviour except for an inflated gravitational constant.

InternalFieldCompare , the offset is substantial for Sculptor for any mass-to-light ratio, for Draco and Carina for high mass-to-light ratios, and for Fornax and Leo II for low mass-to-light ratios. The luminosity of Ursa Minor, Carina, Draco, and Sextans are in the range [ 2,4.1 ] 10^5 L _ and are the least luminous dwarfs in the sample (Table DwarfParams ).

sources.summary: The paper finds Draco strongly favors a cusped dark-matter halo in non-spherical models and identifies it as the best sample target for indirect dark-matter searches.

quotes.quote: Firstly, it is noteworthy that in our non-spherical models, Draco favors a cusped inner slope for its dark matter density profile, which is consistent with an NFW cusp predicted by CDM theory. Even if we consider 95 per cent confidence intervals of the dark matter profile, its inner slope still remains cuspy. Therefore, Draco highly likely has a cusped dark matter halo.

From Figure JDcomp , we conclude that because of having a cuspy dense dark matter halo and of the close distance to the Sun, Draco is the most promising detectable target for an indirect search of dark matter annihilation and decay among all sample dSphs.

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as a low-mass dwarf spheroidal whose stars show low-metallicity chemical evolution and occupy a locus distinct from GE/S.

quotes.quote: In contrast, for most low-mass dwarf spheroidal galaxies (except for Fornax), [ Mg/Fe ] decreases monotonically with increasing [ Fe/H ] .

Based on a compilation of chemical compositions from various works CohenHuang2009,CohenHuang2010,Starkenburg2013 , quote [ Fe/H ] _ knee 1.9 for Sculptor, Ursa Minor, and Fornax and [ Fe/H ] _ knee 2.5 for Draco and Carina see also deboer2014 . The low metallicity limit of our sample prohibits any statement on the existence of a change of slope for the latter two galaxies.

Differences between the loci occupied by GE/S and lower mass satellites are even more pronounced, particularly for Draco, Carina, Sextans and Ursa Minor, whose sample stars are predominantly located outside the ``chemically unevolved'' region.

sources.summary: The paper uses WHAM observations to set deep upper limits on diffuse ionized gas in Draco, finding no emission and discussing limits on gas mass retained by the dwarf spheroidal.

quotes.quote: The Wisconsin Mapper has been used to set the first deep upper limits on the intensity of diffuse emission from warm ionized gas in the Draco and Ursa Minor dwarf spheroidal galaxies. Assuming a velocity dispersion of 15 km s ^ 1 for the ionized gas, we set limits of I_ H 0.024 R and I_ H 0.021 R for the Draco and Ursa Minor dSphs, respectively, averaged over our 1 circular beam. Note that the Draco and Ursa Minor dSphs could contain substantial amounts of interstellar gas, equivalent to all of the gas injected by dying stars since the end of their main star forming episodes 8 Gyr in the past, without violating these limits on the mass of ionized gas.
We find upper limits of I_ H 0.024 R and I_ H 0.021 R for the Draco and Ursa Minor dSphs, respectively, averaged over the 1 circular WHAM beam.

sources.summary: The review says Draco's very metal-poor stars were tested against Draco 119's unusual abundance pattern; follow-up found no equally metal-poor giants and no generally extreme neutron-capture deficiency.

quotes.quote: This begged the question: is the Draco 119 abundance pattern unique, ie. due to some strange inhomogeneous mixing event, or is this pattern found in all very metal-poor Draco stars. A search for equally metal-poor Draco stars, using the Hobby-Eberly telescope, did not turn up any Draco giants as metal-poor as Draco 119; but it did turn up a few stars just a few tenths more metal-rich. By integrating long enough to detect the strong red Ba lines in these stars using the HRS on the Hobby-Eberly telescope, Shetrone et al. (2005) did not find extremely neutron capture poor stars, see Figure 2. The abundance pattern of Draco 119 appears to be a due to inhomogeneous mixing and is not found in all very metal-poor Draco stars.

sources.summary: The review describes a tentative CACTUS gamma-ray excess toward the Draco dwarf galaxy, noting baryon-poor conditions, conflicts with annihilation expectations and EGRET, and a later null reanalysis.

quotes.quote: Some preliminary results of the CACTUS collaboration have recently been interpreted as a possible evidence for a DM annihilation signal from the Draco dwarf galaxy. Because of the reduced amount of baryons in such a small astrophysical system, an exotic origin of the observed O(100) GeV emission was certainly worth being explored. Several authors have investigated this possibility and concluded that the tentative detection was at odds with the expected annihilation signal, as well hard to reconcile with earlier EGRET observations . More recently, a re-analysis of the CACTUS data has shown no evidence for an excess of gamma-rays above 100 GeV .

sources.summary: The paper models Draco as a dark-matter-dominated classical satellite at 80 kpc, with stronger kinematic constraints and a 20% Fermi detection chance under the PL boost model.

quotes.quote: As examples, we apply our algorithm to two particular satellites, both of which are known to be strongly dark matter dominated: the classical satellite Draco, which is located at 80 kpc from the Sun, and Segue 1, which is a newly-discovered satellite at 23 kpc from the Sun.
The kinematic constraints we derive for Draco are much stronger, as this object has been well-studied both from the standpoint of its photometry and kinematics.

Specifically, for the PL boost model, we find 20 of the Draco flux parameter space is detectable with signal-to-noise greater than 3 with five years of Fermi observation (and similarly 13 of the Segue 1 parameter space at the same level).

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as a well-constrained, promising classical dSph and long-favored northern target, although Ursa Minor is marginally better in its J-factor ranking.

quotes.quote: However, while we find Sculptor and Draco to be the next most favorable targets, 2010AdAst2010E..45K names Draco and Carina from his 'simulation-based' approach.

Our analysis places Ursa Minor as the best candidate for the northern sky (marginally better than Draco, which has long been a favourite target of northern hemisphere observatories) and Sculptor for the southern sky, when it comes to a favourable median and low uncertainty in the J factor.

With these criteria in mind, we find three categories: well-constrained and promising (Ursa Minor, Sculptor and Draco), well-constrained but less promising (Carina, Fornax and Leo I), and poorly constrained (Sextans and Leo II).

sources.summary: The paper includes Draco among the classical Milky Way dwarf spheroidals and finds that, under isotropic Jeans modeling, it prefers a cored dark-matter halo.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, even in this limit, not all of the dSphs prefer large constant-density cores: the Sculptor dSph prefers a cusp while Carina, Draco and Leo I prefer cores.
We use a spherical Jeans analyses to constrain the density profile slopes of the eight classical MW dSphs: Carina, Draco, Fornax, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor, Sextans, and Ursa Minor.
Three galaxies (Carina, Draco, and Leo I) demonstrate a preference for a core ( 0 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Suzaku observations of Draco, a dark-matter-dominated dwarf spheroidal, to set moduli decay limits and compare its dark-matter profile with Ursa Minor.

quotes.quote: We present the results of a search for such dark matter particles using spectra extracted from the first deep X-ray observations of the Draco and Ursa Minor dwarf spheroidal galaxies, which are dark matter dominated systems with extreme mass-to-light ratios and low intrinsic backgrounds.
The Ursa Minor and Draco systems, that have among the highest known dark matter surface densities , remain the only dwarf spheroidals observed by Suzaku to date.

The Draco and Ursa Minor profiles are very similar within 600 pc, consistent with an NFW profile with M_ 200 =3-30 10^9 M_ where M_ 200 is the mass encompassing an overdensity, relative to the critical density, of 200 , and a scale radius determined by the WMAP5 mass-concentration relation .

sources.summary: The paper includes a preliminary Draco proper-motion estimate, places Draco among satellites with Magellanic-Stream-like angular momentum, and finds its orbital pole aligned with the common direction.

quotes.quote: For Draco, 2008glv..book..199P have published a new PM estimate based on a preliminary analysis of HST observations but explicitly warned against using this value due to its uncertain nature.

In particular, most of the galaxies which LyndenBell1995 consider to be possible members of the Magellanic Stream (LMC, SMC, Draco, Ursa Minor, Sculptor and Carina) have a specific angular momentum which is close to h = 13.0 kpc km s ^ 1 , the value predicted by LyndenBell1995 .

The orbital poles of at least six satellites (LMC, SMC, Ursa Minor, Leo II, Draco and Fornax) are closely aligned with each other, being inclined by less than 27^ \ from their average direction (which points to [ l,b ] = [ 177^ ,-3^ ] ) \ and close to the minor axis of the VPOS.

sources.summary: Draco is modeled as a Milky Way dSph with a steep inferred inner density slope and is grouped with high-M/L systems whose halos tend to be more flattened.

quotes.quote: In this section, we describe the best-fit dark halo model for each of seven MW dSphs (Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, Leo I, and Leo II) and five M31 dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) and present confidence maps between each parameter.

In Figure  fig:fig2 and  fig:fig3 , we present 68  ( 1 ), 95  , and 99  confidence levels of contours in the two-dimensional plane of Q- _z , _0- _z , _z , b_ halo _z , and i- _z for all sample dSphs: Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, and Leo I are shown in Figure  fig:fig2 , and Leo II, And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII are shown in Figure  fig:fig3 .

In light of these predictions, it is interesting to remark that the dark halos of Fornax, Sculptor, Leo I, and Leo II with relatively low M/L s tend to be less flattened than Carina, Sextans, and Draco with high M/L s.

sources.summary: The paper singles out Draco dSph as unusually halo-massive for its stellar mass, contrasting it with Fornax and suggesting its low stellar-to-halo mass ratio implies little tidal stripping.

quotes.quote: As discussed in Section sec:right , a notable exception is the Draco dSph, which appears to have an unusually high halo mass for its stellar mass.

Furthermore, the stellar-to-total mass ratios of individual dwarf spheroidals show a surprising amount of scatter: Fornax is roughly 100 times brighter than Draco, but appears to inhabit a halo of similar mass.
By contrast, the low stellar-to-halo mass ratio of Draco is more typical of isolated dwarf galaxies in our simulations, suggesting that Draco has not yet experienced strong tidal effects.

sources.summary: The review treats Draco as a classical dark-matter target, contrasting analyses where its null X-ray-line result is either consistent with or excludes the claimed 3.5 keV cluster signal.

quotes.quote: They both looked at the Draco dwarf galaxy since it is a classical target for dark matter searches. In they reported null result from Draco, however they affirm that to be consistent with the 3.5 keV line observed in the stack of galaxy clusters at 95 C.L. In , on the order hand, the authors concluded that the non-observation of a x-ray line in Draco actually excludes the 3.5 keV line at 99 C.L.

sources.summary: The paper reports no detected pulsars in Draco, treats it as a classical dSph with strong J-factor constraints, and predicts faint MSP emission below Fornax.

quotes.quote: No pulsars have been found yet in Ursa Minor, Draco, or Leo I with searches at 350 MHz using the Green Bank Radio Telescope Rubio-Herrera:2013 . Perhaps more importantly, Draco and Ursa Minor, two classical dSphs with large and well constrained J-factors e.g., Geringer-Sameth:2014yza , have estimated MSP fluxes another order of magnitude fainter than Fornax. For dSphs with M_ * > 10^ 4 M_ , the contribution from MSPs within the dSph is predicted to exceed the foreground in most cases (e.g., Fornax, Sculptor, Draco).

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as a dwarf spheroidal comparison object with a flat velocity-dispersion profile, a possible central dip, and contour dents reproduced by the models.

quotes.quote: In Sextans we see a slight drop in velocity dispersion around 1 kpc, while Sculptor, Draco and Fornax show flat profiles their figure 2 Walker2007 .
Some of our models have a central dip in the velocity dispersion profile like in Sextans or Draco.
Furthermore, we can resemble specific properties seen in some dSph, as for example off-centre nuclei (Sextans), secondary density peaks (Ursa Minor) or dents in the contours (Draco).

sources.summary: The paper includes Dra I among six dwarf spheroidal dark-matter targets, highlights it as having robust halo parameters, and reports its J factor differs from a literature value by a factor of four.

quotes.quote: Here we present new estimates of the DM content in the dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSphs; ), which appear to be the best observational targets for indirect DM searches in terms of proximity and lack of background emission . In particular, we focus our analysis on the classical dSph Draco I (Dra I) and the ultra-faint Coma Berenices (CBe), Canes Venatici I (CVn I), Segue 1 (Seg 1), Reticulum II (Ret II) and Triangulum II (Tri II).

In Fig. fig-2 , we also show the profiles of J and D for the dSph with the highest expected DM amount (Tri II) and that with the most robust DM halo parameters (Dra I). ht Our MCMC examination of the first six DM halos in a completely uniform framework of input data allows us to produce J and D values for dSphs in agreement with the literature. The most discrepant result is J(0.5 deg ) for Dra I, which is offset by a factor of 4 from e.g. the value given by .

sources.summary: The paper describes Draco as a bright Milky Way dwarf spheroidal on a closer pericenter orbit, associated with higher dark-matter density and one of the densest subhalos in TBTF modeling.

quotes.quote: The dSphs that have not come close to the Milky Way center (like Fornax, Carina and Sextans) are less dense in DM than those that have come closer (like Draco and Ursa Minor).

The inferred /m spanned a large range from 0.1 - 0.3 cm^2/g (Ursa Minor and Draco) to values greater than 10 cm^2/g (Fornax and Sextans), with a CDM concentration-mass prior from Vogelsberger:2015gpr .

To explain TBTF for the bright MW dSphs in light of the Gaia data using collisionless DM models, one must host Draco and Ursa Minor in the densest subhalos with pericenter distances close to 30 - 40 kpc, while explaining why Carina, Fornax and Sextans are hosted by lower-density subhalos that did not come close to the MW disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as its dwarf-spheroidal case study for dark-matter annihilation radio signals, adopting its density profile and diffusion radius.

quotes.quote: It is thus shown that the radio flux from a dSph like Draco should be observable in about 100 hours at the SKA, for dark matter masses upto 4-8 TeV.

For our analysis we have taken Draco In this paper we have used the dSph Draco to illustrate our points, primarily because the relevant parameters such as the J factor are somewhat better constrained for this object .
For Draco, r_h is taken to be 2.5 kpc .

sources.summary: BALRoGO uses the Draco dSph as a Gaia EDR3 demonstration case for surface-density fitting, membership selection, and color-magnitude-diagram filtering.

quotes.quote: We use Gaia EDR3 data from the two GCs NGC 6752 and NGC 6205 (M 13) along with the Draco dSph galaxy as illustrative examples in our Figures, throughout the paper.
The results of our fits of surface density model plus constant interloper contribution are displayed in Figure fig: sd-fits for NGC 6752, NGC 6205 (M 13) and the Draco dSph, respectively.
The CMD of NGC 6752, NGC 6205 (M 13) and the Draco dSph are presented in Figure fig: cmd-fits , with the 3 (2.5 , for the dSph) contour region displayed as a black thick line.

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as an intact dwarf spheroidal comparison, noting I/II stars, Indus-like process-enhanced metallicities, and a predicted abundance knee near [Fe/H] = -2.1.

quotes.quote: Of the present-day intact dwarf spheroidal galaxies, a mixture of I and II stars has been discovered in three systems: Ursa Minor (UMi), Draco, and Fornax shetrone2001,cohen2009,letarte2010,lemasle2014 .

In metallicity, the Indus stream stars mostly overlap with the process enhanced stars found in Draco and UMi, whereas the I and II stars in the more massive Fornax galaxy generally have higher metallicities, and those in the small ultra-faint, Ret II galaxy are predominantly found at lower metallicities.

A recent abundance re-analysis of high-resolution data for stars in Fornax and UMi has found the knee in these two galaxies to be located at [ Fe/H ] = -1.6 and [ Fe/H ] = -2.2 , respectively, and predicted the location in Draco to be [ Fe/H ] = -2.1 reichert2020 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as a dwarf spheroidal target for COSI dark-matter line searches, using D-factor limits and approximating it as point-like.

quotes.quote: We consider various targets such as the Galactic Center (GC), the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and the dwarf spheroidal galaxy Draco.

To bracket the uncertainty in its determination we consider two cases, one more promising and one less promising, for each target: NFW and Isothermal profiles for LMC (with parameters from ) and GC (taking _ =0.4 GeV/cm^3 and r_s=4.38 kpc (Isothermal) and r_s=24.42 kpc (NFW) ), and upper and lower 68 C.L. limits for the D-factor of Draco derived in .
Given the relatively poor angular resolution of COSI, Draco can be considered as a point-like source.

sources.summary: The paper treats Draco as a classical dwarf plausibly linked to the Magellanic system, tied to the stream plane and a roughly even association probability.

quotes.quote: In particular, four of the classical dwarfs -- Carina, Draco, Fornax and Ursa Minor -- each have a 50 probability of once belonging to the Magellanic system, thus providing a possible explanation for the ``plane of satellites'' conundrum.

Previous work LyndenBell1976 noticed that the dwarf galaxies Draco, Ursa Minor and Sculptor lie close to the plane of the Magellanic gas stream, itself discovered shortly before Mathewson1974 , and proposed that they are tidally stripped satellites of the postulated Great Magellanic Galaxy -- the precursor of the LMC itself.

In particular, four of the classical dSph -- Carina, Draco, Fornax and Ursa Minor -- have a roughly equal chance to either be accreted with the Magellanic system or originate from elsewhere (in case of Carina, the chances are even higher, 2:1 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Draco as a benchmark high-M/L dwarf spheroidal, arguing that similarly dense satellites can survive near the Milky Way and still require substantial dark matter.

quotes.quote: We show that only dwarfs with central dark matter densities as high as those of Draco and Ursa Minor can survive for 10 Gyr in the proximity of the Milky Way: this is naturally achieved within hierarchical models, where the densest objects should have small orbital times due to their early formation epochs.

Moreover, both dSphs and dIrrs are dark matter dominated and show an anti-correlation between their mass-to-light ratio and their luminosity (Mateo 1998): Draco and Ursa Minor, the faintest dSphs ( M_B > -10 ), have M/L > 25 (Lake 1990)) and the dimmest dIrrs, GR8 and SagDIG ( M_B > -12 ) have M/L 30 .

Although not negligible, such tidal effects cannot rule out the need of high dark matter contents in some of the dSphs: objects like Draco and Ursa Minor ( M/L > 25 ) would still have M/L > 12 even with the maximum velocity dispersion enhancement just described.

sources.summary: The paper describes Draco as a dwarf spheroidal with old metal-poor stars, a very high mass-to-light ratio, and extended dark halo models matching its velocity-dispersion profile.

quotes.quote: Ultra-deep HST imaging for Draco and UMi show their stellar populations are indeed indistinguishable from those of old metal-poor globular star clusters, and hence have M/L_V 2 .

Even in the inner regions mass does not follow light, while including outer data commonly we find a most likely global mass to light ratio which is very high, being for Draco 440 , 200 times greater than that for stars with a normal mass function (Fig. 1). The Draco halo models favoured by the data contain significant amounts of mass at large radii, leading to the observed flat to rising velocity dispersion profiles at intermediate to large radii.

sources.summary: Draco is grouped with nearby high-M/L dwarf spheroidals where extra-tidal stars would argue against dominant dark matter and significant dark matter content.

quotes.quote: Carina, Ursa Minor and Draco, on the other hand, cannot contain a dominating dark matter component if the observed 'extra-tidal' stars are located exterior to the tidal radii of these systems.

Even with the large error bars, which are due to the large observational uncertainties in the orbital and structural parameters, figure 1 clearly demonstrates that in this case Carina, Draco and Ursa Minor could not be dark matter dominated (see also Moore 1996).
The existence of 'extra-tidal' stars in Carina, Draco and Ursa Minor therefore would demonstrate that these dSphs cannot contain significant amounts of dark matter.

gmw_00000547.name: B2016+112
gmw_00000547.overview: B2016+112 is a gravitationally lensed type-II quasar system at a redshift of approximately 3.27 [arXiv:astro-ph/0701021, arXiv:astro-ph/0006382]. The system is lensed by a giant elliptical galaxy at redshift 1.01, producing multiple radio components and images [arXiv:astro-ph/0701021].

sources.summary: The paper describes B2016+112 as a gravitationally lensed type-II quasar system with AGN images A and B, component C, and a primary lens galaxy D producing quadruply imaged radio structure.

quotes.quote: The gravitational lens system B2016+112, discovered in 1981 in the MIT-Green Bank 6-cm survey, shows three radio components A, B and C (see Figure 1 left). The overall integrated spectrum of this system is that of a Gigahertz Peaked Spectrum source. Components A and B, separated by 3.4 arcseconds, are associated with the lensed images of an AGN at redshift z=3.273 ( ; see Figure 1, centre).

Component C, which is the brightest radio source in the field, lies 2 arcseconds away to the south-east of B and is extended in the E-W direction. It is associated with a diffuse extended object in the infrared. A giant elliptical galaxy D at z=1.01 ( ), lying close to the centroid of A, B and C, observed in K and I bands ( ), acts as the primary lens (see Figure 1, right).

In this model B2016+112 is interpreted as a quadruply imaged lens system, in which the diamond caustic crosses the radio source such that just part of the ``counter-jet'', host galaxy and narrow line regions are imaged at A, B and C (where C consists of two partial images, C1 and C2). The complete source, including all radio components and the optical nucleus, is only doubly imaged (A and B).

sources.summary: The paper treats MG2016+112 as a z=3.26 triple quasar whose complex third image motivates the search for an additional cluster-scale gravitational lens.

quotes.quote: A mysterious system in this respect is the triple quasar MG2016+112 ( z=3.26 ). It was discovered by Lawrence et al. \ lawrence84 and studied in more detail by Schneider et al. \ schneider85,schneider86a in the optical and by Garret et al. \ garret94,garret96 in the radio. The third image named C presents a complex structure, suggesting some differential magnification of the source within each image.

gmw_00000548.name: Gr 8
gmw_00000548.overview: Gr 8 is a nearby, low-metallicity dwarf irregular galaxy located in the foreground of the Virgo cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0603241, arXiv:1206.6916]. Its neutral hydrogen emission is triple-peaked and enclosed in a cross-shaped halo, with three completely broken holes detected in its gas disk [arXiv:1501.06709, arXiv:2006.01735].

sources.summary: The paper studies diffuse ionized gas in Gr 8, describing it as a nearby low-metallicity dwarf irregular whose line ratios and photon leakage differ from spiral-galaxy DIG.

quotes.quote: We have studied the spectral characteristics of the Diffuse Ionized Gas in two irregular galaxies with low metallicities and intermediate Star Formation Rates: ESO 245-G05 and Gr 8. The [ OIII ] /H in these galaxies is higher than in the DIG of spiral galaxies but not as high as in other irregular galaxies previously studied, such as IC 10 and NGC 6822. The [ NII ] /H and [ SII ] /H ratios have very small values, indicating the absence of shocks as the ionization source for this gas. This ionization can be explained in both galaxies with photon leakage from the H ii regions as the only source. The percentage of photons escaped from the H ii regions is small in ESO 245-G05, of only 35 , but varies from 35 up to 60 in Gr 8.

In the present investigation we study two dwarf irregular galaxies with low chemical abundances and intermediate SFRs: Gr 8 and ESO 245-G05. The first one is a nearby galaxy at a distance of D = 2.24 Mpc (Hidalgo-G amez Olofsson 1998) dominated in the optical by two large H ii regions. The metallicity is very low (12+log(O/H) = 7.4 0.1 , Hidalgo-G amez Olofsson 2002) and also the SFR, which is 2.12 10^ 3 M _ yr ^ 1 (Hunter Elmegreen 2004).

sources.summary: DDO 155 is mapped as a dwarf irregular target with two distinct emission regions, similar but offset emission, and a southwest component missing in one line.

quotes.quote: We present far-infrared spectral line observations of five galaxies from the \ sample: DDO 69, DDO 70, DDO 75, DDO 155, and WLM. While most studies of dwarfs focus on bright systems or starbursts due to observational constraints, our data extend the observed parameter space into the regime of low surface brightness dwarf galaxies with low metallicities and moderate star formation rates. Our targets were observed with \ at the \ 158 m, \ 63 m, \ 88 m, and \ 122 m emission lines using the PACS Spectrometer. These high-resolution maps allow us for the first time to study the far-infrared properties of these systems on the scales of larger star-forming complexes.

DDO 155 has two distinct \ emission regions: one just east of the image center, and the other roughly 30 \ to the southwest. The \ emission originates in similar locations, though slightly offset. The \ again appears somewhat similar, but the southwest component is not detected, and the \ signal comes from just north of the SW component of .

sources.summary: The paper builds a GR8-like dwarf model, using its faint luminosity, gas kinematics, high halo density, and mass-to-light ratio to test dense-dwarf transformation into spheroidals.

quotes.quote: Examples of very faint dIrrs are found in the LG; both GR8 and SagDIG have M_B > -12 . It turns out that galaxies that faint should correspond to halos with M_ 200 < 10^ 9 M_ , and that such halos form typically at z 2 in cold dark matter cosmogonies (Lacey Cole (1993, 1994)). We build a model resembling the GR8 dwarf using the kinematics of the gas at large radii to construct its global potential. At 2-3 disk scale lengths the neutral hydrogen in GR8 is pressure supported, with a peak velocity dispersion 12-13 km/s (Carignan et al. 1990).

This value is slightly higher than inferred for GR8 ( 0.07 M_ /pc^2 ) but similar to that inferred for Draco and Ursa Minor (Lake 1990; Mateo 1998). The mass-to-light ratio at the optical radius ( 3 r_h ) is 32, like that of GR8 not counting the contribution of the gaseous disk. The rotation curve of the ``GR8'' model is shown in Figure 3.

sources.summary: The paper divides GR8's H I into three star-forming clouds, finding asymmetric profiles in the southwestern and northern clumps, few Gaussian departures in the eastern clump, and spatial coincidence between star formation and asymmetric H I.

quotes.quote: The three prominent HI clouds of GR8 each contain young massive stars and emission; the stellar content of these regions is described by dp1998 . The southwestern clump contains the highest concentration of massive main sequence stars and is therefore interpreted as the youngest region of star formation activity in the galaxy. This HI clump shows asymmetric profiles with h_4 > 0 (Figures gr8h3hiha and gr8h4hiha ), but only in the southern half of the clump near the star formation activity.

The eastern HI clump has the highest concentration of blue helium-burning stars, which are somewhat older and less massive than main sequence stars of comparable luminosity. This clump shows remarkably few departures from Gaussian profile shape. The northern HI clump has the smallest massive star content, and it is interpreted as the oldest of the three regions of recent star formation activity. Line profiles throughout this entire HI clump have high degrees of asymmetry (the highest h_3 values measured in the entire sample) and h_4 > 0 .

sources.summary: The paper classifies D646-7/UGC 8091 as an irregular dwarf with a Gaussian H I profile, no clear rotation, and only linewidth velocity indicators.

quotes.quote: D646-7 (or UGC 8091) is classified as an irregular dwarf galaxy for which estimate a distance of 2.1 0.6 Mpc, using the tip of the red giant branch. The global \ profile of D646-7 has a clear Gaussian shape. The galaxy lacks clear signs of rotation, both in the velocity field and in the major-axis position-velocity diagram.

Thus, only 0 and 0 remain as indicators for the maximum rotation velocity. The optical inclination of D646-7, derived by , is 55 , which is in good agreement with the (uncorrected) inclination of the \ disk ( 51 ). However, given that the galaxy is not well resolved, we use the optical inclination in our analysis.

sources.summary: The paper highlights GR8 among the lowest-luminosity nearby XMPs, describing this low-surface-brightness subset as unusually gas-rich and inefficient at converting H i gas into stars.

quotes.quote: We call attention to the six sources with the lowest g band luminosity -- UGC2684, DD053, LeoA, SextansB, UGCA292 and GR8. These are all nearby (D < 6 Mpc), low-surface brightness galaxies, that also turn out to be XMPs.

We find an anti-correlation between the M _ H i /L _g ratio and the luminosity, whereby fainter XMPs are more gas-rich than brighter XMPs. The result suggests that the less luminous sources have converted a smaller fraction of their H i gas into stars. However, our sources deviate from the observed correlation found for dwarf galaxies (cyan line; bottom; Fig. 5; Staveley-Smith, Davies Kinman 1992) particularly at low luminosities, which correspond to the low-surface brightness, nearby galaxies UGC2684, DD053, LeoA, SextansB, UGCA292 and GR8. In this low luminosity regime, the XMP galaxies appear to be 10 to 100 times more gas-rich than typical dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper compares DDO 155 with Dutta et al., reports steeper spectral indices, and says its large-scale power spectra are not power-law enough for a strong comparison.

quotes.quote: Dutta et al. \ (2009b) study has two galaxies in common with our sample: DDO 210 and DDO 155. \ They found that DDO 210 has power spectral indices of 2.1 0.6 and 2.3 0.6 for velocity-integrated and single channel (1.7 km s ^ 1 ) velocity slices, respectively, and DDO 155 has power spectral indices of 0.7 0.3 and 1.1 0.4 for velocity-integrated and single channel velocity slices, respectively. \ These are compared to our values of 3.66 0.05 and 2.17 0.09 for DDO 210, and 3.97 0.15 and 2.59 0.12 for DDO 155.

The power spectra of DDO 155 are not power-law for linear scales above 1.5 times the beam size, and thus the quality of power-law fitting is very poor (Figure fig1 ). \ We notice that the power spectra of DDO 155 determined by Dutta et al. \ (2009b) show similar behavior on large scales. \ So DDO 155 may not be a good case for comparison.

sources.summary: GR 8 is described with triple-peaked H I inside a cross-shaped halo, a kinematic axis misaligned from the disk, radial/circular gas motions, and V-shaped high-resolution structure.

quotes.quote: GR 8 (also known as DDO 155, UGC 8091 or VIII Zw 222) From Young et al. (2003; their Fig. 3) and Begum Chengalur (2003; their Fig. 2, 3 and 4), with the VLA, Begum et al. (2006; their Fig. 9, 12 and 13,), with the GMRT, and Ott et al. (2012; their Fig. 23), from the VLA ANGST. At low and intermediate resolution (30 x 30 and 25 x 25 ), the overall H 1 emission is triple-peaked, enclosed in a "cross-shaped" halo (PA _ HI 165 ^ ), where the central triple-peak emission is "V-shaped". The kinematical axis (PA _ kin 80 ^ ) is misaligned with both the H 1 and the optical disk emission (PA _ opt 130 ^ ). The velocity field can be modeled by a combination of circular plus radial motions of the gas, showing distortions in the outskirts. Intermediate resolution (18.6 x 18.1 and 14.8 x 14.6 ) and high resolution ( 6 and 4 x 3 ) H 1 images mimic the triple-peaked "V-shaped" structure (PA _ HI 165 ^ ) seen at lower resolution. These peaks are slightly misaligned relative to the position of the three brighter star-forming regions in the optical disk.

sources.summary: The paper treats GR 8 as a low-luminosity, low-metallicity dwarf irregular target; its expected PN population was negligible and the survey found no planetary nebulae.

quotes.quote: Here we describe the results of the observations of the next three targets: the dwarf irregular galaxies IC 1613 (morphological type Ir V according to vdb00 , hereafter vdB00), \ (Ir IV-V; vdb00 ), and 8 \ (dIrr; mateo ).
No PN have been identified in 8 \ jacoby .

As described above, IC 1613, , and 8 \ are low-luminosity and low-metallicity galaxies, so their expected PN number is very small. From the V-band luminosity vs. number of PNe diagram (Fig. 2 of M03), and assuming a search as deep as that made for Sex B, i.e. complete up to mag _ OIII =24.5 (M03), we infer that the expected observable number of PN for IC 1613 is 10 and 3 for WLM; while none is expected in GR8. GR 8 and WLM The result in 8 \ was expected from the low- luminosity of this galaxy and is in agreement with the survey of jacoby which did not find any PN.

sources.summary: The paper reports one Cepheid in GR8, derives a Cepheid distance modulus with extinction and PLR uncertainties, and compares it with earlier Cepheid and TRGB results.

quotes.quote: GR 8 In GR8 we only find one Cep star with m_R=21.47 0.07 and P=15.44 d (Fig. o25_gr8_lcs ). We derive a distance modulus of m-M=26.45 0.07 0.06_ ZP ( 0.25_ PLR ) taking into account a galactic extinction of A_R=0.07 1998ApJ...500..525S . Since this distance is based on a single detection the full intrinsic scatter of the PLR has to be considered as an additional uncertainty. This Cep star had already been discovered by 1995AJ....109..579T with m_ <> =22.12 and P=16.166 d. They derived a distance modulus of m-M=26.75 0.35 which 1998AJ....116.1227D claim to be consistent with their HST based TRGB observations.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 08091 as a foreground Virgo dwarf irregular galaxy, reports consistency with previous H I measurements, and derives a low H I mass.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1258+14 : UGC 08091 is a dwarf irregular galaxy at a distance of about 2 Mpc. It lies in the foreground of the Virgo cluster. Our \ value agrees well with previous \ measurements. We derive an \ mass of 8.5 10^6 .

sources.summary: The paper reports that DDO 155 has three completely broken H I disk holes, two overlapping, and gives its scale height and disk radius.

quotes.quote: DDO 155 DDO 155, Gr 8, LSBC D646-07, PGC 44491 or UGC 8091 has three completely broken holes in its \ disk with two of them overlapping. We estimated the scale height of the galaxy about 140 pc and the disk radius about 0.7 kpc. This galaxy is located in the Local Group at a distance of about 2.2 Mpc.

gmw_00000549.name: ESO 245-G05
gmw_00000549.overview: ESO 245-G05 is a Magellanic-type dwarf irregular galaxy located in the Sculptor group [arXiv:astro-ph/0603241, arXiv:astro-ph/0302045]. It features a central bar-like region [arXiv:astro-ph/0302045], low oxygen abundance [arXiv:astro-ph/0603241], and a modest star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0603241].

sources.summary: The paper presents ESO 245-G05 as a Sculptor-group dwarf irregular with low oxygen abundance, modest star formation, and DIG ionized mainly by photon leakage from H II regions.

quotes.quote: We have studied the spectral characteristics of the Diffuse Ionized Gas in two irregular galaxies with low metallicities and intermediate Star Formation Rates: ESO 245-G05 and Gr 8. The [ OIII ] /H in these galaxies is higher than in the DIG of spiral galaxies but not as high as in other irregular galaxies previously studied, such as IC 10 and NGC 6822. The [ NII ] /H and [ SII ] /H ratios have very small values, indicating the absence of shocks as the ionization source for this gas. This ionization can be explained in both galaxies with photon leakage from the H ii regions as the only source. The percentage of photons escaped from the H ii regions is small in ESO 245-G05, of only 35 , but varies from 35 up to 60 in Gr 8.

ESO 245-G05 is one of the Sculptor group dwarf galaxies. The oxygen content is also low ( 7.6 7.8 with small variations along the bar, see Hidalgo-G amez et al. 2001) and the SFR is 1.1 10^ 2 M _ yr ^ 1 (Miller 1996).

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 245--005 as a Magellanic-type irregular galaxy whose central bar-like region contains about 11000 resolved stars and has a TRGB distance of 4.43 Mpc.

quotes.quote: This irregular galaxy of Magellanic type has an angular dimension of 3 8 3 4 and a radial velocity of V_ LG = 308 km s ^ 1 . In the WFPC2 image of its central bar-like part we detected about 11000 blue and red stars. We determined the I band TRGB to be at 24 22 0 21 , yielding a distance of 4.43 0.45 Mpc.

gmw_00000550.name: RX J1242.6-1119A
gmw_00000550.overview: RX J1242.6-1119A is an optically inactive galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.05 [arXiv:astro-ph/0402468, arXiv:2103.15442]. It hosted an exceptionally luminous, very soft X-ray outburst [arXiv:2103.15442] that was localized to its nucleus [arXiv:0704.3480] and attributed to a tidal disruption event [arXiv:astro-ph/0402468, arXiv:2103.15442].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RX J1242--1119A as the optically brighter non-active galaxy coincident with a fading X-ray flare, supporting a tidal-disruption event from its central black hole.

quotes.quote: The target of the present study, RX J1242--1119, was first detected by ROSAT in 1992 (Komossa Greiner 1999) during a pointed observation with the Position Sensitive Proportional Counter (PSPC). At that time it showed a very soft X-ray spectrum with kT_ bb 0.06 keV, and an X-ray luminosity of L_ x 9 10^ 43 ergs s^ 1 , which is exceptionally large given the absence of any signs of Seyfert activity of RX J1242--1119 in ground-based (Komossa Greiner 1999) and Hubble Space Telescope ( HST , Gezari et al. 2003) optical spectra. The association with one of the two, previously unstudied non-active galaxies at redshift z=0.05 located in the X-ray position error circle (named RX J1242--1119A and RX J1242--1119B for lack of a better designation) however, remained uncertain because of the large ROSAT positional uncertainty, in this case 40 ^ (Figure 1).

The Chandra data allow us to locate precisely the counterpart of the X-ray source. We find that the X-ray emission peaks at coordinates R.A.= 12^ h 42^ m 38. ^ s 55 , decl.= 11^ o 19^ 20. ^ 8 (equinox J2000). Within the errors, this position is coincident with the center of the optically brighter of the two galaxies, RX J1242--1119A (Figure 1). An offset in position of 1^ in R.A. can be traced to residual uncertainties in the absolute pointing accuracy of the X-ray telescope. Neither Chandra nor XMM-Newton detected any other X-ray source within the ROSAT X-ray position error circle, including from the optically fainter galaxy RX J1242--1119B.

sources.summary: The paper treats RX J1242.6-1119A as a normal-galaxy nuclear X-ray flare candidate, emphasizing ROSAT detection, point-source localization, and lack of optical AGN or NVSS radio activity.

quotes.quote: RX J1242.6-1119A This is an interesting source and its detailed observational results can be found in komgre99 and komhal04 . RX J1242.6-1119A was observed two times by ROSAT, with the first time during RASS(ROSAT all-sky survey) during DEC 1990 -- JAN 1991. The second time was a pointed observation with PSPC (position sensitive proportional counter). It was not detected in the RASS (this gives count rate < 0.015 cts/s) but it became visible in the pointed observation with the maximum count rate given by 0.3 cts/s komgre99 .

Since both Chandra and XMM observations suggest that the detection is a point source, we believe that the detected X-ray was still related to the flare at the nucleus rather than the other parts of the galaxy komhal04 . Careful studies in the observation by different telescopes in the optical band suggests that there is no AGN activities in the galactic centre see, e.g. halgez04 . There is also no radio detection for this source by NRAO VLA Sky Survey(NVSS) Akom02 .

sources.summary: The review presents RX J1242-1119 as an inactive galaxy with an exceptionally luminous, very soft ROSAT outburst, quiescent optical spectrum, and decade-long Chandra fading characteristic of a strong TDE case.

quotes.quote: A very luminous X-ray outburst was discovered from the inactive galaxy RX J1242-1119 (at z = 0.05) with ROSAT during a 'serendipitous' pointed observation. Its X-ray spectrum was extremely soft, one of the steepest ever identified among galaxies, and among the steepest in the ROSAT data base, with a photon index of _ x = 5.1 0.9 KomossaGreiner99 . At a soft X-ray luminosity of 4 10^ 44 erg/s (a conservative lower limit, assuming no absorption in the host galaxy itself, and without applying a bolometric correction), this event was exceptionally luminous. Optical spectroscopy of the host galaxy, both ground-based and with the HST, reveals a quiescent, in-active galaxy, with no emission lines detected at all down to deep limits KomossaGreiner99,Gezari:2003a . During the RASS itself, RXJ1242-1119 was not detected, implying an initial amplitude of variability of a factor of > 20 , and a rise-time of < 1.5 years.

Deep Chandra follow-ups (cf next section) then revealed the fading of the X-ray emission from the TDE by a factor of up to > 1000 Komossa:2004ar,Halpern:2004a,Komossa:2005a more than a decade after its high-state. Given its extreme properties and deep follow-ups, this event continues to be one of the best cases of a TDE identified so far Komossa:2004ar .

gmw_00000551.name: NGC 5905
gmw_00000551.overview: NGC 5905 is a nearby barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2103.15442] featuring a long thin bar, a bright classical bulge [arXiv:1607.01889], and circumnuclear star formation [arXiv:1304.2235]. It is known for a giant, soft X-ray outburst [arXiv:astro-ph/9809037] attributed to the tidal disruption of a star by its central supermassive black hole [arXiv:1304.2235].

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 5905 as an exceptional variable Seyfert-like galaxy: ROSAT detected a bright, soft X-ray outburst, later observations found quiescence and no broad Balmer component, and models remain ambiguous.

quotes.quote: One particularly interesting exception merits mention here: NGC 5905 bkd96,kb99 . Bright X-ray emission ( L_ X = 7 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 ) was detected from NGC 5905 in 1990 July by the ROSAT all-sky survey. Follow-up observations over the subsequent months and years failed to redetect the source, implying that the quiescent flux is at least two orders of magnitude fainter than the outburst.

But follow-up optical spectroscopy, both from the ground and with HST , failed to reveal any broad component to the Balmer emission lines kb99,ghk+03 . A variety of models have been proposed to explain the properties of the NGC 5905 outburst (e.g., kb99,lnm02 ). In the AGN context, kb99 explored the possibility of a variable absorbing cloud along the line of sight. In this warm absorber model, the Seyfert nucleus becomes visible by ionizing the ambient medium, making it transparent to soft X-rays (much like a NLS1). This naturally explains the relatively steep X-ray spectrum observed in NGC 5905 ( 5 ), but the presence of a significant dust column is required to hide the broad-line region in the optical.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 5905 as a nearby barred spiral whose soft ROSAT nuclear outburst, long decline, HII-type spectrum, and SMBH-mass estimates fit a tidal disruption interpretation.

quotes.quote: NGC 5905 was first noticed due to its luminous, soft ( kT = 0.06 keV) X-ray outburst with (lower limit on the) peak luminosity in the soft X-ray band of L_ x,peak = 7 10^ 42 erg/s during the RASS This luminosity is even higher, if a powerlaw model is fit, and if there is absorption from the event's host galaxy. KomossaBade:1999a . It remained bright for at least 5 days (the time interval while its position was repeatedly scanned during the RASS) increasing in luminosity to the observed peak (Fig. 1). Given its daily coverage for almost a week, it has one of the best-covered early lightcurves of the non-radio-emitting X-ray TDEs. X-rays then declined on the timescale of months to years, as observed in multiple re-observations with ROSAT (Fig. 2). Within the 5 arcsec positional error (ROSAT HRI), the X-rays came from the center of this nearby barred spiral galaxy (z=0.011; Fig. fig:x:rosat_results_mosaic ).

Ground-based optical spectroscopy before and after the flare has led to a starburst (HII-type) classification of the host galaxy of NGC 5905 KomossaBade:1999a . An HST observation after the flare showed evidence for faint enhanced [ OIII ] emission from the core of the galaxy Gezari:2003a - either long-lived or excited by the flare itself. Using scaling relations between host galaxy and central SMBH mass for elliptical galaxies Ferrarese:2000a , an upper limit on the SMBH mass of NGC 5905 of a few times 10 ^8 was estimated; near the upper limit for the tidal disruption of a solar-type star and as such could imply a spinning SMBH.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5905's giant soft X-ray outburst, finding an HII-type, non-Seyfert optical spectrum and considering tidal disruption by a supermassive black hole as an explanation.

quotes.quote: NGC 5905 is one of the very few galaxies the other three objects are the Seyfert galaxies IC 3599 (Brandt et al. 1995, Grupe et al. 1995a) E 1615+061 (Piro et al. 1988) and WPVS007 (Grupe et al. 1995b) that underwent a giant X-ray outburst, with a change in photon countrate of a factor 100. The outburst spectrum is both, very soft and luminous (Bade, Komossa Dahlem 1996). Remarkably, the optical pre-outburst emission line spectrum of NGC 5905 is that of an HII-type galaxy, i.e. does not show any signs of Seyfert activity. One exciting explanation of the X-ray observation is that we may have witnessed the tidal disruption of a star by a supermassive black hole (SMBH) residing in the nucleus of this galaxy.

A giant X-ray outburst of NGC 5905 was discovered during the (Tr umper 1983) survey observation (Bade, Komossa Dahlem 1996). NGC 5905 showed a high countrate during this first observation. The countrate then declined by at least a factor of several within months and was down by a factor 100 two years later. The X-ray spectrum during the outburst was very soft (with photon index 4.0 when fit by a powerlaw).

sources.summary: The paper interprets NGC 5905 as a non-active HII galaxy with a giant, soft X-ray outburst whose decline and luminosity favor tidal disruption by a central supermassive black hole.

quotes.quote: The giant X-ray outburst in NGC 5905 -- a tidal disruption event ? Stefanie Komossa ^ 1 , Norbert Bade ^2 ^1 Max-Planck-Inst. extraterrestrische Physik, 85740 Garching, Germany skomossa@xray.mpe.mpg.de ^2 Hamburger Sternwarte, 21029 Hamburg, Germany NGC 5905 is one of the very few galaxies the other three are the Seyfert galaxies IC 3599 (Brandt et al. 1995, Grupe et al. 1995a) E 1615+061 (Piro et al.

1995b) that underwent a giant X-ray outburst, with a change in photon countrate of a factor 100. The outburst spectrum is both, very soft and luminous (Bade, Komossa Dahlem 1996). Our high-resolution follow-up optical spectroscopy of NGC 5905 does not reveal any signs of Seyfert activity. At present, this makes NGC 5905 the only non-active galaxy among the X-ray outbursting ones.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 5905 as a starburst galaxy with a large nuclear X-ray flare, repeated ROSAT observations, and ambiguous Chandra emission from the nucleus or starburst region.

quotes.quote: NGC 5905 NGC 5905 is not non-active since HII lines can be found from the optical spectrum. However, as discussed in halgez04 , due to the huge variation in luminosity, tidal disruption event is still the favoured model for the flare. Therefore we included this source here for discussion. NGC 5905 was observed six times by ROSAT, with the first three times observed during RASS, the next two times were pointed observations by PSPC and the last one by HRI (High Resolution Imager) badkom96,kombad99 .

NGC 5905 is observed again by Chandra in OCT 2002. However, the image is a diffused source, with all photons below 1.5keV halgez04 . It is impossible for us to determine if the photons positioned at the nuclei of the galaxy came from the flare or from the star burst region.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 5905 as a barred spiral galaxy with a luminous soft ROSAT X-ray flare whose fading lightcurve matches tidal-disruption expectations.

quotes.quote: NGC 5905 was first noticed due to its luminous, soft ( kT=0.06 keV) X-ray emission with peak luminosity in the soft X-ray band of L_ x,peak = 7 10^ 42 erg/s during the RASS. It remained bright for at least 5 days (the time interval its position was repeatedly scanned during the RASS) increasing in luminosity to the observed peak. X-rays then declined on the timescale of months to years (Fig. 3).

Within the errors, the X-rays came from the center of this nearby barred spiral galaxy ( z=0.011 ; Fig. 2). While the X-ray spectrum was initially very soft, it had hardened significantly ( _ x = -2.4 ) 3 years later, when re-observed with ROSAT . The decline of its X-ray lightcurve is well consistent with the predicted t^ 5/3 law, as first reported based on its ROSAT observations (Komossa Bade 1999) and confirmed with Chandra (Halpern et al. 2004). All observations of this event are in very good agreement with tidal disruption theory (Bade et al. 1996, Komossa Bade 1999).

sources.summary: The paper attributes velocity-field deviations in NGC 5905 to non-circular motions in its large-scale bar, while rejecting a secondary-bar explanation for the central isophote twist.

quotes.quote: The velocity fields of stars and ionized gas exhibit deviating from PA_0 at =1''-9'' , in the opposite direction with respect to the PA s of the inner isophotes. Hence they should be caused by non-circular motions in the large-scale bar. The observed slower [ OIII ] ionized gas rotation compared with the value and the difference of the values in these lines are most likely connected with shock fronts in the bar edges, as in the case of NGC 3786 (Sect. sec_3786 ).

Friedli et al. ( ) showed that the turn of the inner isophotes, at <10'' , is caused by a secondary bar. Nevertheless, they used an obviously wrong value of PA_0=45^ . Note that a relatively small value of the ellipticity of the inner isophotes, and the coincidence of the isophotal PA with PA_0 does not allow us to explain the central structure with a secondary bar. Most probably, the twist of the central isophotes at =5''-10'' is caused by the transition of the surface brightness distribution character from a bar-dominated to a central prolate bulge or circumnuclear disk.

sources.summary: The paper reports extended radio emission at NGC 5905, but after filtering short baselines finds no convincing compact point source at the optical nucleus and assigns a conservative upper limit.

quotes.quote: NGC 5905 An extended radio source is found at the position of NGC 5905. The source has a fitted size of 5 \ 4 and a total flux density of 6.7 mJy. Filtering out baselines shorter than 80 k , we find some residual structure but no convincing evidence for a point source at the location of the optical nucleus.

The residual structure has a peak intensity of 200 Jy beam ^ 1 and is offset from the location of the optical nucleus. The image rms within the ROSAT error circle is 42 Jy. We estimate a 3 upper limit of 126 Jy for a point source at that location but conservatively assign an upper limit of 200 Jy.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5905 as a TDE host with no strong AGN evidence; late X-ray, infrared, and radio data instead point to declining flare emission and nuclear/circumnuclear star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 5905 is one of the few galaxies with no prior evidence for an active galactic nucleus (AGN) in which an X-ray flare was detected by the ROSAT All Sky Survey (RASS) in 1990-91. Follow-up studies showed that the X-ray flare was due to the tidal disruption of a star by the massive black hole in the center of the galaxy. In this study we present analysis of late-time follow-up observations of NGC 5905 using Chandra archival data, Spitzer archival data, GMRT 1.28 GHz radio observations and VLA 3 GHz and 8 GHz archival data. The X-ray image shows no compact source that could be associated with an AGN. Instead, the emission is extended -- likely due to nuclear star formation.

Our studies thus show that (i) NGC 5905 has a declining X-ray flux consistent with a TDE, (ii) the IR flux is dominated by nuclear star formation, (iii) the nuclear radio emission observed from the galaxy is due to circumnuclear star formation, (iv) no compact radio emission associated with a radio afterglow from the TDE is detected.
