sources.summary: The paper finds Sextans' cleaned velocity-dispersion profile changes substantially, its M_300 falls below the common 10^7 scale, and interloper removal lowers its MOND M/L to a borderline value.

quotes.quote: However, for Sextans and Sculptor, the losVDs have changed considerably. The typical error bars on each losVD bin in are 1.5 , but we have used fewer bins, thus increasing the number of stars per bin and decreasing the uncertainty on each losVD value. Additionally, the three sets of losVDs identified by the caustic technique, found using different star masses, do not systematically differ by more than 0.5 until the last data point for Carina, Sextans and Sculptor (see Figure fig:veldisp_caus ).

One can see that our sample of five appears consistent with a constant value of 10^7 within 300 pc although Sextans falls a factor of two short. We also overplot the enclosed mass from fits made by Angus (2010; where the original losVDs of walker07 were fitted with the same DM density used here) and the trend for the enclosed mass to decrease after interloper removal is obvious, except for Leo I and Sextans.
After removing the interloper stars identified by the caustic technique, Sextans' M/L has dropped to 2.7 0.5, but Carina still has a rather high M/L of 5.9 ^ +1.4 _ 1.1 in solar units.

sources.summary: The paper flags Sextans as a spatial-coverage exception with a radial metallicity gradient and higher contamination risk; its MDF is neither leaky-box nor ram-pressure stripped.

quotes.quote: Our slitmasks span at least the half-light radii for all galaxies in our sample except Sextans. Therefore, our samples represent at least half of the stellar populations---and typically much more than half---in all of the galaxies except for Sextans, which has a radial metallicity gradient of 0.35 dex kpc ^ 1 bat11 .

The Sextans spectroscopic sample is expected to be more contaminated with non-members than the other two dSphs because it has the lowest galactic latitude. Even if all of the discarded stars belonged to Sextans, the bias against metal-poor stars is at most 3.1 .
Sextans, Ursa Minor, Draco, and Canes Venatici I are neither Leaky Boxes nor ram pressure stripped.

sources.summary: The paper says Sextans abundances mainly come from Battaglia2011 and Kirby2010, with additional high-resolution samples and generally insignificant Ti differences among common stars.

quotes.quote: The majority of the abundance data for Sextans are supplied by Battaglia2011 and Kirby2010 . The two papers produce abundance data for 315 stars, 174 of which are based on the metallicity scale using CaT equivalent width Battaglia2011 . The rest of the abundance data are available with high-resolution spectra using the Subaru/HDS 6 and 1 stars, respectively Aoki2009,Honda2011 , the Keck/HIRES 5 stars Shetrone2001 , and the VLT/UVES 2 stars Tafelmeyer2010 . The adopted solar abundances are not common in any of the papers, except for two papers Battaglia2011,Shetrone2001 that are not available in the literature. There are four stars in common for derived Ti abundances in Sextans between Shetrone2001 and Kirby2010 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Sextans as a classical dwarf-spheroidal satellite in a homogeneous very metal-poor stellar-parameter sample for comparing dwarf-galaxy and Milky Way halo populations.

quotes.quote: We present a homogeneous set of accurate atmospheric parameters for a complete sample of very and extremely metal-poor stars in the dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSphs) Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Sextans, Fornax, Bo otes I, Ursa Major II, and Leo IV.

The comparison between ultra-faint, classical dwarf spheroidal galaxies (UFDs, dSphs), and the Milky Way population offers a fantastic opportunity to probe different galaxy masses, star formation histories, levels of chemical enrichement. Both nucleosynthetic processes and galaxy formation models largely benefit from the diversity of the populations sampled that way.

We selected 36 stars in total in the classical dSphs Sculptor (Scl), Ursa Minor (UMi), Fornax (Fnx), and Sextans (Sex) and the ultra-faint dwarfs Bo otes I, Ursa Major II (UMa II), and Leo IV (Table Tab:spectra ). This sample covers the 4 Fe/H < -1.5 metallicity range.

sources.summary: The paper derives Sextans' inner-region star formation history, finding no metallicity gradient, a short early burst, and supernova feedback likely removing gas and halting prolonged star formation.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We present the star formation history (SFH) of the Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy based on deep archive B , I photometry taken with Suprime-Cam at Subaru telescope focusing our analysis on the inner region of the galaxy, fully located within the core radius. Within the errors of our SFH we have not detected any metallicity gradient along the considered radial distance interval. As a main result of this work we can state that the Sextans dwarf spheroidal stopped forming stars less than 1.3 Gyr after Big Bang in correspondance to the end of the reionization epoch. We have been able to constrain the duration of the main burst of star formation to 0.6 Gyr. From the calculation of the mechanical luminosity released from supernovae (SNe) during the brief episode of star formation, there are strong indications that SNe could have played an important role in the fate of Sextans, by removing almost completely the gas component, so preventing a prolonged star formation.

sources.summary: The paper reports accretion or merger signatures in Sextans, including metallicity-dependent structure, a shell-like overdensity, anomalous ring-like kinematics, and very low stellar mass.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of clear observational signs of past accretion/merger events in one of the Milky Way satellite galaxies, the Sextans dwarf spheroidal (dSph). These were uncovered in the spatial distribution, internal kinematics and metallicity properties of Sextans stars using literature CTIO/DECam photometric and Magellan/MMFS spectroscopic catalogues. We find the spatial distribution of stars to vary as a function of the colour/metallicity, being rather regular and round for the blue (metal-poor) red giant branch and main-sequence turn-off stars but much more elliptical and irregularly shaped for the red (metal-rich) ones, with a distinct ``shell-like'' overdensity in the northeast side. We also detect kinematic anomalies, in the form of a ``ring-like'' feature with a considerably larger systemic line-of-sight velocity and lower metallicity than the rest of stars; even the photometrically selected component with a regular looking spatial distribution displays complex kinematics. With a stellar mass of just 5 10^ 5 M_ , Sextans becomes the smallest galaxy presenting clear observational signs of accretion to date.

sources.summary: Paper models Sextans as a classical dwarf spheroidal using action-based stellar and dark-halo distribution functions, fitting number-density and velocity-dispersion profiles with anisotropic spherical models.

quotes.quote: Here we model five further dSphs: Carina, Leo I, Sculptor, Sextans and Ursa Minor, with the aim to prove that the use of the DF ( for:df1 ) can be extended to the whole population of classical dSphs. We present spherical, anisotropic models, with separate DFs for the stellar and the halo components, which just fit the dSph number density profiles, given a certain orbital anisotropy.

The upper panels of Fig. fig:dSphs show that for all four values of the best DF provides an excellent fit to the observed number density profiles of the four galaxies. The lower panels shows the observed velocity dispersion profiles of the galaxies alongside the predictions for each value of . Table tab:dSphs gives the values of the parameters and of ^2 for the best-fitting models of Carina, Leo I, Sextans and Ursa Minor.

sources.summary: The paper uses Gaia EDR3 to identify outer candidate member stars around Sextans, reporting eight radial-velocity matches, seven RR Lyrae candidates beyond the limiting radius and possible extended halos or tidal debris.

quotes.quote: We use Gaia EDR3 data to identify stars associated with six classical dwarf spheroidals (Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextans, Sculptor, Fornax, Carina) at their outermost radii, beyond their nominal King stellar limiting radius. For all of the dSphs examined, we find radial velocity matches with stars residing beyond the King limiting radius and with > 50 astrometric probability (four in Draco, two in Ursa Minor, eight in Sextans, two in Sculptor, twelve in Fornax, and five in Carina), indicating that these stars are associated with their respective dwarf spheroidals (dSphs) at high probability. We compare the positions of our candidate ``extra-tidal'' stars with the orbital tracks of the galaxies, and identify stars, both with and without radial velocity matches, that are consistent with lying along the orbital track of the satellites. Cross matching with publicly available catalogs of RR Lyrae, we find one RR Lyrae candidate with > 50 astrometric probability outside the limiting radius in each of Sculptor and Fornax, two such candidates in Draco, nine in Ursa Minor, seven in Sextans, and zero in Carina. Follow-up spectra on all of our candidates, including possible metallicity information, will help confirm association with their respective dSphs, and could represent evidence for extended stellar halos or tidal debris around these classical dSphs.

sources.summary: The paper detects a significant Sextans distance gradient and finds oblate models require intrinsic ellipticity above 0.47, with gradients mainly along the semi-minor axis.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We combine the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey (DECaLS) DR8 photometry with Gaia photometry to study the 3-D structure of Bootes I, Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextans and Sculptor dwarf galaxies using blue horizontal branch (BHB) stars as distance indicators. We construct a new colour-absolute magnitude of BHB stars that we use to measure the distance gradients within the body of the dwarf galaxies. We detect a statistically significant non-zero gradient only in Sextans and Sculptor. Through modeling of the gradient and 2-D density of the systems by triaxial Plummer models we find that the distance gradients in both dwarf galaxies are inconsistent with prolate shape, but compatible with oblate or triaxial shapes. In order to explain the observed gradients, oblate models of Sextans and Sculptor need to have a significant intrinsic ellipticity larger than 0.47 for Sextans and 0.46 for Sculptor.

sources.summary: The paper models Sextans with 327 stellar tracers and finds a broadly uncertain inner-slope distribution, with a cuspy peak but a strong wing toward shallower slopes.

quotes.quote: We apply our model to four Local Group dwarf spheroidal galaxies and find results consistent with those obtained with the Jeans modelling based code GravSphere : the Fornax dSph has a strong indication of possessing a central DM core, Carina and Sextans have cusps (although the latter with large uncertainties), while Sculptor shows a double peaked PDF indicating that a cusp is preferred, but a core can not be ruled out.

The selected galaxies are Carina, Sextans, Fornax and Sculptor, for which we further use the center position, velocity, ellipticity and half-light radius as compiled in . To build our input PDFs, we considered only those stars with a 90 or higher probability of being part of the galaxy and we took the mean value of the line-of-sight velocity for those stars with multiple measurements. In total, we considered 460 stars for Carina, 1353 for Fornax, 809 for Sculptor and 327 for Sextans, and we used their projected x-y positions and line-of-sight velocities.

Sextans presents a relatively large uncertainty in the inner slope value, as depicted by the quite broad PDFs, with a broad peak around = -1.25 and a strong right wing that does not fall below 10 of the peak value until it reaches = -0.68 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes large-radius Sextans member stars, confirms them as metal-poor, identifies three previously unknown chemical signatures, and finds multiple heavy-element nucleosynthesis channels in the dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: We present stellar parameters and chemical abundances of 30 elements for five stars located at large radii (3.5--10.7 times the half-light radius) in the Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy. We selected these stars using proper motions, radial velocities, and metallicities, and we confirm them as metal-poor members of Sextans with 3.34 [ Fe/H ] 2.64 using high-resolution optical spectra collected with the Magellan Inamori Kyocera Echelle spectrograph.
We identify three chemical signatures previously unknown among stars in Sextans.
Heavy Elements in Sextans Multiple heavy-element nucleosynthesis channels were present in the \ dwarf galaxy. At least three are apparent among just the five stars in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper measures RGB-star metallicities for Sextans as one of four nearby dSph galaxies, finding no stars below -3 dex and a metal-poor tail differing from the Galactic halo.

quotes.quote: As part of the Dwarf galaxies Abundances and Radial-velocities Team (DART) Programme, we have measured the metallicities of a large sample of stars in four nearby dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSph): Sculptor, Sextans, Fornax and Carina. The low mean metal abundances and the presence of very old stellar populations in these galaxies have supported the view that they are fossils from the early Universe. However, contrary to naive expectations, we find a significant lack of stars with metallicities below 3 dex in all four systems. This suggests that the gas that made up the stars in these systems had been uniformly enriched prior to their formation. Furthermore, the metal-poor tail of the dSph metallicity distribution is significantly different from that of the Galactic halo.

sources.summary: Sextans is described as old-dominated with population and metallicity gradients; its CG97 spectroscopic-photometric mismatch is attributed to very metal-poor stars beyond the Dartmouth isochrone metallicity range.

quotes.quote: Sextans shows a population gradient based on its horizontal branch morphology (Harbeck et al. ), as well as a metallicity gradient, where the metal--rich stars are more centrally concentrated and have colder kinematics than the metal--poor ones (Battaglia et al. ).

This is not the case for Sextans, where the difference of the median spectroscopic and the photometric metallicity amounts to 0.48 dex, based on 173 stars (Battaglia et al. ) (white histogram in the middle panel of Fig. sl_4mdf ). This mismatch between the two median metallicity values for Sextans can be explained if one considers that the individual Ca T spectroscopic metallicities on the CG97 metallicity scale include metallicity values as metal--poor as 4 dex (Battaglia et al. ), while the individual photometric metallicities were restricted to 2.5 dex, which is the most metal--poor value of metallicity provided for the Dartmouth isochrones. Therefore, the different selection criteria in terms of metallicity ranges used for the metallicities on the CG97 metallicity scale and the photometric metallicities may account for this large difference, which further suggests that such a comparison may not be appropriate for a galaxy with as metal--poor stars as in Sextans.

sources.summary: The paper includes Sextans among bright Milky Way dwarf spheroidals used to test SIDM subhaloes, finding its large half-light radius inconsistent with the 15 most massive simulated subhaloes.

quotes.quote: The dSphs sample consists of the 9 galaxies used in Boylan2011b : Fornax, Leo I, Sculptor, Leo II, Sextans, Carina, Ursa Minor, Canes Venatici I and Draco, selected with the criterion L_V>10^5 M _ .

Looking at Figure fig:sub_vcirc we see that at least three of the dSphs (Fornax, Sextans and Canes Venatici I, that have a value of r_ 1/2 1 kpc) are inconsistent with the circular velocity profile of the 15 most massive subhaloes in all our SIDM simulations, particularly for the cases with a velocity-dependent cross section. This however, does not mean that there are no subhaloes in these simulations that are a good match to these galaxies. These subhaloes exist but are considerably less massive than the ones we show in Figure fig:sub_vcirc and are more affected by the limited resolution of our simulations.

sources.summary: The paper includes Sextans as a smaller dwarf-spheroidal sample, deriving HFS-corrected manganese abundances for five stars and finding sub-solar Mn/Fe ratios across the four studied galaxies.

quotes.quote: We provide manganese abundances (corrected for the effect of the hyperfine structure) for a large number of stars in the dwarf spheroidal galaxies Sculptor and Fornax, and for a smaller number in the Carina and Sextans dSph galaxies. Abundances had already been determined for a number of other elements in these galaxies, including and iron-peak ones, which allowed us to build [ Mn/Fe ] and [ Mn/ ] versus [ Fe/H ] diagrams. The Mn abundances imply sub-solar [ Mn/Fe ] ratios for the stars in all four galaxies examined.
Mn abundances in a fourth dSph galaxy, Sextans (5 stars), was based on only one to three Mn lines.

sources.summary: The paper models Sextans as external-field dominated, with effective dynamical mass following baryonic mass and predicted mass-to-light ratios far below published estimates.

quotes.quote: Sextans Sextans' distance to the Galactic Centre is not much larger than that of Scuptor, but in comparison it is much fainter (by a factor of 1/5 ), bringing it into the external field-dominated regime: The effective dynamical mass follows the baryonic mass and M_ dyn /L 9.7 M_*/L Sextans . The choice of the exact density model and the exact total luminosity are of minor importance (within certain limits of course), and the stellar mass-to-light ratio as well as the strength of the external field are entirely determining the resulting effective dynamical mass-to-light ratio. Also Sextans' PDM density profile is clearly cored. The dynamical mass-to-light ratios we derived for Sextans under the assumptions of Milgromian dynamics to describe gravity correctly are far below the values found by Walker2008 and Strigari2008 , whose errors cover the wide range from 108 to 373 / .

sources.summary: The paper places Sextans in the top five J and D rankings and calls it the brightest classical dwarf-spheroidal target for decaying dark matter, while noting possible cored-halo evidence.

quotes.quote: The Sextans, Ursa Minor and Draco `classical' dSphs feature in the top five of both the J and D factor rankings. The larger kinematic samples in these objects make the Jeans modelling more robust (as indicated by their smaller confidence intervals in figures fig:J_results and fig:D_results ). However, in the case of Sextans 2013ApJ...777...65L,2004MNRAS.354L..66K and Ursa Minor 2014MNRAS.442.1718P,2005ApJ...630L.141K , it has been suggested that the presence of kinematic substructures indicate that the dark matter halos of these objects are cored rather than cusped.
For decaying DM, Sextans appears as the brightest `classical' dSph at _ ^ D r_h /d , while UMa 2 remains the brightest `ultrafaint' target.

sources.summary: The paper selects 325 Sextans members from Cicuendez probabilities, finds the result stable against probability cuts, and notes a modest Helmi difference for this large galaxy.

quotes.quote: Sextans (I) We use the catalog of Cicuendez_18 with continuous probabilities. With our standard cuts we select 325 members. The proper motion changes by less than 0.1 when we change the spectroscopic probability cut to P > 0.7. Our motion differs by 1.6/0.7 (in R.A./Dec.) from the motion of Helmi_18 , which might happen by chance or due to additional systematic differences due to the large angular size of this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper includes Sextans in its axisymmetric Jeans analysis of classical dwarf spheroidals, finding its soliton-plus-NFW parameters broadly consistent with soliton-only modeling.

quotes.quote: More precisely, we solve axisymmetric Jeans equations and apply them to the kinematic data of the eight classical dSphs: Draco, Ursa Minor, Carina, Sextans, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor and Fornax in order to investigate the global shape of ULA dark halo as well as to constrain on particle mass of ULA dark matter.

The Galactic Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy Data In this work, we adopt the photometric and spectroscopic data of the resolved member stars in the eight luminous dSphs in the Milky Way: Draco, Ursa Minor, Carina, Sextans, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor, and Fornax. This is because each have 200 stars with line-of-sight velocities from spectroscopy and well-constrained stellar distributions from photometry, so that these sample volumes might be enough to obtain more stringent limits on the mass range of m_ than those from other fainter dSphs such as ultra faint dwarf galaxies.

From these results, most of the dSphs (Draco, Carina, Sextans, Leo II and Sculptor) have larger r_ than b_ and r_ 90 , and the five parameters (Q,r_c,m_ , _z,i) for these galaxies are roughly consistent with these estimated by the soliton-only models.

sources.summary: The paper models Sextans as similar to Sculptor but lower-efficiency and lower-metallicity; its Mg and Mn abundance trends are best matched by all SN Ia channels plus boosted SN Iax.

quotes.quote: The models for Sculptor and Sextans are very similar; both are formed by a rapid infall and star formation with a strong outflow. Since the star formation efficiency in Sextans is lower than in Sculptor, the average [ Fe/H ] of the MDF is 0.3 dex lower, which is probably due to the mass difference of the systems.

In Figure fig:sextans for Sextans, the observed [ Mg/Fe ] ratios cannot be reproduced without sub-Ch-mass SNe Ia (green short-dashed lines), but are in reasonably good agreement with the model including all three SN Ia channels (red solid lines). The observed [ Mn/Fe ] can be better explained if the SN Iax rate is boosted by 10 times (blue long-dashed lines).

sources.summary: The paper describes Sextans as showing possible two-knee magnesium behavior, evidence for accretion or merger history, and a short star-formation episode that drained its gas.

quotes.quote: The Sextans dSph galaxy seems to show an indication of having two knees when looking at the magnesium abundances (dashed line in Fig. fig:mg_evolution , cf.
Cicuendez2018 found that Sextans may have undergone a galaxy accretion or merger event.

The lack of metals before the onset of type Ia SNe in Sextans could be also explained by a short main episode of star formation. Bettinelli2018 found that the main episode of star formation lasted only 0.6 Gyr and ended 12.9 Gyr ago, which supports the hypothesis that Sextans quickly drained most of its gas reservoir.

sources.summary: The paper groups Sextans with the less dense classical dwarf spheroidals, allowing a cored dark-matter density within uncertainties and remaining possible after prior testing.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, the remaining sample of dSphs (Carina, Sextans, Sculptor, and Fornax) favors smaller and thus has less dense than the other dSphs which have cuspy dark matter halos. In particular, Sextans, Sculptor, and Fornax permit =0 , i.e. a cored dark matter density within their 95 per cent confidence intervals.

It is found from this figure that the galaxies having a cusped dark matter halo like Draco and Ursa Minor are not so much affected by new prior, whilst the effect of new prior makes Fornax and Sextans less dense core. Therefore, we bear in mind that Fornax and Sextans are possible to have a cored dark matter density.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Sextans as an observed outlier with a relatively flat old-age metallicity gradient and an unusual profile that steepens beyond 1.5 half-light radii.

quotes.quote: One clear outlier in the left panel of Figure fig:obs_compare is Sextans (pink triangle). This galaxy has a fairly flat gradient compared to simulated galaxies of similar (old) age. The metallicity profile of Sextans is somewhat unusual, as can be seen by the yellow line in the right panel with a fairly flat slope out to 1.5R_ 1/2 accompanied by a steep gradient beyond that. In keeping with the _z definition used elsewhere, we have defined it using the slope out to 2R_ 1/2 , but had we defined it out to larger radii we would have measured a steeper slope, more in line with the global trend.

sources.summary: The paper presents Sextans as a dwarf with an extensive core-halo stellar profile, little evidence for tidal stripping, and a dark-matter interpretation consistent with other dwarf estimates.

quotes.quote: The Sextans dwarf galaxy provides a further clue, as its stellar profile is like the isolated dwarfs, with an extensive core-halo structure and a relatively small transition gap of _ C-H 1.5 , see Figure 3b. This suggests, in the context of DM, that Sextans lacks significant tidal stripping, and so it is interesting that several claims have been made that Sextans lacks any sign of tidal interaction from its smooth morphology and internal dynamics and concluded that Sextans is relatively newly bound to the Milky Way (Roderick et al. 2016 Okamoto et al. 2017). This conclusion is also supported by the relatively large Galactocentric distance of Setans, 90 kpc and near circular GAIA based orbit (Fritz et al. 2018). We have previously interpreted the velocity dispersion profile of Sextans in the context of DM with an independent The halo mass distribution and boson mass are relatively well defined here, despite the flat input priors indicating a reliable result.Jeans analysis (Broadhurst et al. 2020) under the assumption of no significant stripping, finding a similar boson mass to other dwarf based estimates of 10^ 22 eV.

sources.summary: DELVE DR1 data around the Sextans dwarf spheroidal show its stellar distribution, central-density variations, and a color-magnitude diagram reaching the subgiant branch.

quotes.quote: In sextans , we show an example of the DELVE DR1 data surrounding the Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy, which is located at a distance of 84.7 0.4 Vivas:2019 . In the left (middle) panel, we plot the smoothed spatial distribution of stars (galaxies) after applying a selection based on g as presented in classification . We note similar variations in the central stellar density of Sextans as were reported by Roderick:2016 . In the right panel, we present a color--magnitude diagram for the stars located within 10 of Sextans, demonstrating that the DELVE DR1 g band depth provides high-precision photometry down to the subgiant branch of this system.

sources.summary: Sextans is described as exceptional among nearby dwarf spheroidals: its extra-tidal stars lie within the inferred tidal radius, and avoiding disruption requires a dominant dark matter component.

quotes.quote: Evidence for extra-tidal stars associated with Sextans has been detected by Gould et al. (1992). However, the Gould et al. stars, associated with Sextans, are located within the tidal radius determined by Irwin Hatzidimitriou (1995).
Sextans has a cut-off radius which is consistent with the assumption of a large M/L .
If Sextans is a gravitationally bound satellite, then it must contain a dark matter halo as otherwise its tidal radius would be a factor of five smaller than its cut-off radius.

sources.summary: The paper describes Sextans as the least constrained density-profile case, with smaller M_300 than most dSphs, and places it in the poorly constrained target category.

quotes.quote: The least constrained () (less pronounced narrowing of the confidence limits) is that of Sextans, for which the range where useful data can be found is clearly the smallest compared to other dSphs (see Fig. fig:momentprofiles ).

For instance, Sextans and Carina are dSphs with smaller M_ 300 with respect to the other ones (see Tab. tab:res_par6 ); consequently they are located below the dashed blue line in the top panel of Fig. fig:Jd_gamma_fixed .

With these criteria in mind, we find three categories: well-constrained and promising (Ursa Minor, Sculptor and Draco), well-constrained but less promising (Carina, Fornax and Leo I), and poorly constrained (Sextans and Leo II).

sources.summary: The paper finds Sextans differs from the other modeled dwarf spheroidals: isotropic Burkert-halo fits drive its core radius much smaller because its dispersion profile requires tangential anisotropy.

quotes.quote: With the exception of Sextans, the halo parameters obtained for our sample are consistent with those in Table  tab:mcmcresults within the quoted uncertainties. When we restrict ourselves to isotropic models, the best-fit r_0 for Sextans falls to the significantly smaller value of 47 pc. This is consistent with the fact that Sextans is unique in requiring tangential anisotropy to obtain a good fit to the dispersion profile - the best-fit isotropic model does not match the profile of Sextans interior to 200 pc.

sources.summary: The paper models Sextans as a classical dwarf spheroidal and reports that its isotropic Jeans solutions prefer a mild cusp but remain too broad for strong conclusions.

quotes.quote: We use a spherical Jeans analyses to constrain the density profile slopes of the eight classical MW dSphs: Carina, Draco, Fornax, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor, Sextans, and Ursa Minor.

Carina (black dotted) demonstrates a strong preference for living in a cored dark matter halo ( 0 ), while Fornax (red dashed) and Sextans (orange dot-dashed) prefer moderate cusps ( 0.7 ), but their distributions are too wide to draw any strong conclusions.
Sculptor strongly favors a cuspy inner profile ( 1 ), while Fornax and Sextans prefer mild cusps ( 0.7 ), albeit with large uncertainties.

sources.summary: The paper finds Sextans dSph has a binary fraction consistent with Milky Way field binaries and compatible with Milky-Way-like binary population parameters.

quotes.quote: We find that the Fornax, Sculptor, and Sextans dSphs are consistent with having binary populations similar to that of Milky Way field binaries to within 68 confidence limits, whereas the Carina dSph is remarkably deficient in binaries with periods less than 10 years.
By this standard, Fornax, Sculptor, and Sextans all have binary fractions consistent with Milky Way field binaries to within 68 confidence limits.

This set of parameters lies squarely within the allowed parameter region for the Fornax, Sculptor, and Sextans dSphs, indicating that a Milky-Way like binary population is compatible with the data in those galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses the first available Sextans proper-motion estimate and says its orbital pole can come close to the common satellite-plane direction despite large uncertainties.

quotes.quote: There have been updated PM measurements for several MW satellite galaxies and in addition the first PM estimates have been published for the three remaining `classical' satellites Leo I, Leo II and Sextans.
We nevertheless include their data because they provide the only currently available PM estimate for Sextans.
Within their considerable uncertainties, the orbital poles of Sextans and Carina come close to this common direction, too.

sources.summary: Sextans is modeled as a Milky Way dSph; the paper maps its dark halo parameters and groups it with high-M/L systems whose halos tend to be more flattened.

quotes.quote: In this section, we describe the best-fit dark halo model for each of seven MW dSphs (Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, Leo I, and Leo II) and five M31 dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) and present confidence maps between each parameter.

In Figure  fig:fig2 and  fig:fig3 , we present 68  ( 1 ), 95  , and 99  confidence levels of contours in the two-dimensional plane of Q- _z , _0- _z , _z , b_ halo _z , and i- _z for all sample dSphs: Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, and Leo I are shown in Figure  fig:fig2 , and Leo II, And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII are shown in Figure  fig:fig3 .

In light of these predictions, it is interesting to remark that the dark halos of Fornax, Sculptor, Leo I, and Leo II with relatively low M/L s tend to be less flattened than Carina, Sextans, and Draco with high M/L s.

sources.summary: The paper flags Sextans as a dwarf spheroidal whose dark-halo estimates are affected by sample size, peculiar kinematics, large tidal radius, and incomplete outer spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: Here, we note that the observational results from Sextans, Andromeda II and VII dSphs are significantly affected by the sizes of data samples and their peculiar stellar kinematics. Since Sextans might have a very large tidal radius, the currently available spectroscopic data for this galaxy are quite incomplete in the outer region. Moreover, HC2015b demonstrated that the lack of kinematic data sample in the outer region of a stellar system gives a large impact on determining dark halo parameters, especially the axial ratio of a dark halo and its velocity anisotropy (see Figure 12 in their paper).

sources.summary: The paper discusses Sextans as a classical dwarf spheroidal whose VMP stars share the alpha plateau and participate in massive-dwarf Sr/Mg abundance behavior.

quotes.quote: We show that once the NLTE effects are properly taken into account, all massive galaxies in our sample, that is, the MW halo and the classical dSphs Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Sextans, and Fornax, reveal a similar plateau at [ /Fe ] 0.3 for each of the process elements: Mg, Ca, and Ti.
On the other hand, lower [ Ca/Fe ] and [ Ti/Fe ] than [ Mg/Fe ] ratios were reported by 2004AJ....128.1177V and 2010ApJ...719..931C for the VMP stars in the Sextans and Ursa Minor dSphs.

For Sr/Mg, there are two clear sequences at [ Mg/H ] 2.6 , one scattering around [ Mg/H ] 0.37 , which gathers the MW halo stars and the most massive (classical) dwarfs, such as Sextans, Ursa Minor and Sculptor, while the UFDs Leo IV, UMa II, and Bo otes I keep remarkably consistent and low abundance ratios, at the level of [ Sr/Mg ] 1.3 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Sextans as a dwarf spheroidal comparison case showing a slight outer velocity-dispersion drop, a central-dip analogue, and an off-centre nucleus feature.

quotes.quote: In Sextans we see a slight drop in velocity dispersion around 1 kpc, while Sculptor, Draco and Fornax show flat profiles their figure 2 Walker2007 .
Some of our models have a central dip in the velocity dispersion profile like in Sextans or Draco.
Furthermore, we can resemble specific properties seen in some dSph, as for example off-centre nuclei (Sextans), secondary density peaks (Ursa Minor) or dents in the contours (Draco).

sources.summary: The paper describes Sextans as a bright Milky Way dwarf spheroidal with lower inferred dark-matter density, sensitivity to its large half-light radius, and a lower-density subhalo orbit.

quotes.quote: The dSphs that have not come close to the Milky Way center (like Fornax, Carina and Sextans) are less dense in DM than those that have come closer (like Draco and Ursa Minor).
From figure fig:corr_rho_pericenter , we see that Fornax, Carina and Sextans have lower _ 150 values in cISO fits than NFW ones, because their half-light radii are considerably larger than 150 pc.

To explain TBTF for the bright MW dSphs in light of the Gaia data using collisionless DM models, one must host Draco and Ursa Minor in the densest subhalos with pericenter distances close to 30 - 40 kpc, while explaining why Carina, Fornax and Sextans are hosted by lower-density subhalos that did not come close to the MW disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses Sextans as an example of a less dense, diffuse satellite that SIDM core expansion and tidal effects could form in a low-density subhalo.

quotes.quote: The overall effect for /m 3 \ cm^2/g is to increase the range of inner densities, potentially explaining the observed diversity of Milky Way satellites, which include compact systems like Draco and Segue 1 that are dense in dark matter, and less dense, diffuse systems like Sextans and Crater II.

On the other hand, those that undergo core expansion due to self-interactions and tidal effects will have low central DM densities and large core sizes: diffuse satellites like Crater II and Sextans would form this way in SIDM models. Thus, the large range of predicted core sizes in our work could provide a natural explanation for the large range of observed stellar sizes for the MW satellites.

sources.summary: The paper models Sextans as a low-luminosity dwarf where the external field is highly relevant, MOND predicts Newtonian-like behavior, and weak internal gravity leaves negligible phantom-dark-matter offset.

quotes.quote: Figure InternalFieldCompare clearly shows that for the dwarfs Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextans, and Carina, which are located in the top left corner for low stellar mass-to-light ratios, the EFE is expected to be substantially more relevant than for the dwarfs Leo I and Leo II, located in the bottom right corner for high stellar mass-to-light ratios.
When the external field is much larger than the internal field (Carina, Draco, Sextans, and Ursa Minor), MOND predicts a Newtonian-like behaviour except for an inflated gravitational constant.

In Ursa Minor and Sextans, which have the lowest baryonic density, the internal gravitational field is so weak that the phantom dark matter density show a negligible offset compared to the offsets of all the remaining dwarfs.

sources.summary: The paper places Sextans among low-mass dwarf spheroidals with evidence for a low-metallicity knee and a chemical locus distinct from GE/S.

quotes.quote: In contrast, for most low-mass dwarf spheroidal galaxies (except for Fornax), [ Mg/Fe ] decreases monotonically with increasing [ Fe/H ] .
Indeed, previous studies of stars in Draco, Sextans and Ursa Minor shetrone2003vlt , Sculptor Hill2019 , and Sextans theler2020chemical suggest the presence of a ``knee'' at [ Fe/H ] -2.

Differences between the loci occupied by GE/S and lower mass satellites are even more pronounced, particularly for Draco, Carina, Sextans and Ursa Minor, whose sample stars are predominantly located outside the ``chemically unevolved'' region.

sources.summary: The paper uses Sextans as a dwarf-spheroidal comparison, citing its modest ellipticity, low central density, and central young stellar component as properties reproduced by tidal stirring.

quotes.quote: Ellipticities of LG dSphs range from the extreme value of 0.8 in Sagittarius and 0.65 for Ursa Minor to 0.2-0.3 for Sextans, with the average being around 0.4 (Mateo 1998).

Sextans, Sagittarius and Fornax) , namely 0.01 < _0 < 0.1 M_ /pc^3 (Mateo 1998) The remnant of GR8 is comparable to the densest dSphs, Draco, Sculptor and Ursa Minor, having _0 > 0.1 M_ /pc^3 (Lake 1990).

Due to the bar driven inflow, young stars in the remnant would be principally located in its central part. This is what is observed in many LG early type dwarfs, with Fornax, Carina, Sextans and NGC147 being well known examples (Grebel 1999).

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sextans dwarf as a Milky Way satellite whose extended stellar equilibrium, lack of disruption, and low velocity dispersion challenge MOND-like scaling between dwarf galaxies and globular clusters.

quotes.quote: For example, NGC 2419, the largest cluster of the Milky Way, is as distant as and 1-2 times as luminous as the (2.5-4) 10^5 dwarfs Carina/Sextans, which has an equilibrium of stars extending to 1000-3000pc radii at a distance of about 90kpc from the Milky Way. A baryonic universe predicts that NGC 2419 should resist the tide of the Milky Way as well as the Sextans dwarf does on 3000pc scale; Sextans shows no sign of being disrupted (Grebel et al. 2000).

The cores of many Galactic globulars are as hot as dwarf galaxies with central velocity dispersion 8 km/s (cf. Fig. dispersion ), and some dwarf galaxies, e.g., Sextans (Kleyna et al. 2004) are colder than most globulars.

sources.summary: The paper includes Sextans among bright dwarf spheroidals with velocity-dispersion profiles, notes its large tidal radius, and predicts it is much fainter than Ursa Minor and Draco.

quotes.quote: We consider six of the brighter dSphs in our study: Ursa Minor, Draco, Sculptor, Fornax, Carina and Sextans. All of these galaxies have measured velocity dispersion profiles based on the line-of-sight velocities of 200 stars, which may be used to constrain their the dark matter halo potentials.
We can discern this behavior for Sextans, which has a large r_t .
Fornax and Sculptor are a factor of 10 fainter, while Carina and Sextans are fainter by a factor of 100 .

gmw_00000559.name: J0802+2552
gmw_00000559.overview: J0802+2552 is a quasar that exhibits positive detections of microvariability [arXiv:1605.09424]. Morphological classifications suggest the galaxy is an irregular elliptical or in a merging state [arXiv:1605.09424], featuring an asymmetric conical [O III] morphology with a 500 pc outflow [arXiv:1802.06184].

sources.summary: The paper reports microvariability in J0802+2552 on both nights and all filters, and notes Galaxy Zoo morphology votes favoring an irregular elliptical or merging state.

quotes.quote: J0802+2552 was observed during the first half of the night in both cases. As we were observing just before the last quarter, the Moon was not affecting our observations. The data from the first observing night, February 21 2014, were calibrated using 9 stars from the field.

We have detected variability at a significance level 0.001 during this observing night. During the second night of the run, we have observed in three filters ( g' , ' and i' ). Overall we conclude positive detection of variability.

The second iteration of the morphology classification marked J1316+4452 as a spiral with a bulge and medium winded arms with notable irregularity, while J0802+2552 was marked as an irregular elliptical with many votes in favor of a merging state.

sources.summary: 2MASX J080252 shows an asymmetric conical [O III] morphology, likely host-disk extinction of the opposite cone, a 500 pc outflow, and disturbed gas to 700 pc.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J080252 This QSO has an asymmetric, conical [ O III ] morphology pointing north of the nucleus. Kinematics are obtained from the nucleus to distances > 1 kpc north, with emission-line fluxes rapidly becoming fainter south of the nucleus, consistent with the opposite cone being extinguished by the host disk. Rapid differences in FWHM and velocity between nucleus and immediately off-nucleus measurements are due to blending of the blueshifted, offset component, as seen in Figure fig:linefit , at off-nuclear positions. R _ out is measured as the extent of the multicomponent emission lines, which extend along the STIS slit to the northeast approximately 500 pc. Single-component, broad FWHM ( > 250 km s ^ 1 ) emission-lines near systemic are observed to extend to an additional 200 pc, for an R _ dist of 700 pc.

gmw_00000560.name: J0843+3549
gmw_00000560.overview: J0843+3549 is a quasar target that exhibits a highly disturbed and irregular morphology [arXiv:1605.09424]. The system is quite extended and appears to be a composite of two merging targets with a north-south tail [arXiv:1605.09424].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0843+3549 as highly disturbed and extended, likely composite or merging, making aperture photometry unreliable and preventing conclusions on variability.

quotes.quote: J0843+3549 is a target with highly disturbed morphology. The irregular morphology presents a problem for the aperture photometry we are using in all the other cases. The target is quite extended, therefore to include it completely within the aperture is not feasible. Aperture diameter containing this target is approximately 45.6 arcsec (120 px). Such a big aperture is unsuitable for the stars.

However the target, as shown in Figure Fig1 , seems to be a composite of two merging targets. We focus on the brighter (southern) target. SExtractor treats the whole source as two, the tail (in north-south direction) and the quasar itself, therefore it seem sensible to use smaller aperture enclosing only one of the targets The aperture photometry used by SExtractor counts the pixels above the detection threshold in the given aperture.

gmw_00000561.name: J1258+5239
gmw_00000561.overview: J1258+5239 is a quasar and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1605.09424]. Although optical monitoring detected variability in the i-prime filter during one night, there is currently insufficient evidence to confirm microvariability in the source [arXiv:1605.09424].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1258+5239 variable only in the i-prime filter during one moonlit night, concluding that evidence for microvariability is insufficient.

quotes.quote: J1258+5239 was observed during the second part of the night, when the Moon had already risen. We have obtained 85 observations in g' filter during the first night. The aperture photometry was performed with a diameter of 34.2 arcsec (90 px).

The variability is detected only in i' filter, at the significance level of 0.001. Considering that the variability was detected only during one night in one filter, we conclude that there is not enough evidence to confirm variable behavior in the source. Light curves for both observing nights of J1258+5239 are shown in figure Fig6 . As the microvariability was detected only in one filter, we conclude that we have not found enough evidence for microvariations during our observations of this source.

gmw_00000562.name: J1316+4452
gmw_00000562.overview: J1316+4452 is a broad-line active galaxy and type 2 quasar that exhibits variability [arXiv:1605.09424]. Morphological classifications identify it as a spiral galaxy with a central bulge, medium-winded arms, and irregularity [arXiv:1605.09424].

sources.summary: The paper observes J1316+4452 under moonlit conditions, finds second-night variability in two filters, and reports Galaxy Zoo morphology consistent with a spiral bulge and irregularity.

quotes.quote: J1316+4452 was observed during the second half of the nights, with the same conditions of lunar illumination as J1258+5239. Aside from our targeted source, there is a galaxy present in the field of view, southeast from the target as seen in Fig. Fig1 ). It is of roughly similar brightness as our target.

The enhanced F-test on both, accompanying galaxy (J1316+4452b) and J1316+4452 itself results in negative detection of microvariability. We have followed the same strategy as for the other targets and during the second observing night, we were circulating g' , ' and i' filters. We have obtained 30 exposures of the target in each filter.

As the seeing improved compared to the first night, we have used aperture of diameter 20.8 arcsec (55 px). The statistical analysis result in detection of variability at the significance level 0.001 in both, broad-line galaxy and the type 2 quasar. The second iteration of the morphology classification marked J1316+4452 as a spiral with a bulge and medium winded arms with notable irregularity, while J0802+2552 was marked as an irregular elliptical with many votes in favor of a merging state.

gmw_00000563.name: J1316+4452b
gmw_00000563.overview: J1316+4452b is a broad-line galaxy located southeast of the type 2 quasar J1316+4452 [arXiv:1605.09424]. Statistical analysis of the galaxy shows a detection of optical variability at a high significance level [arXiv:1605.09424].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1316+4452b as a broad-line galaxy near J1316+4452, applies the same tests, and detects second-night variability at high significance.

quotes.quote: Aside from our targeted source, there is a galaxy present in the field of view, southeast from the target as seen in Fig. Fig1 ). It is of roughly similar brightness as our target. It is classified as a broad line galaxy in SDSS-III eis . We have applied the same statistical tests on the galaxy as on the target.

During the first observing night, we have obtained 84 images of the field (hence 84 observations of both, targeted type 2 quasar and accompanying galaxy) in g' filter. The enhanced F-test on both, accompanying galaxy (J1316+4452b) and J1316+4452 itself results in negative detection of microvariability. The statistical analysis result in detection of variability at the significance level 0.001 in both, broad-line galaxy and the type 2 quasar.

gmw_00000564.name: Malin 1
gmw_00000564.overview: Malin 1 is a prototypical giant low-surface-brightness galaxy [arXiv:1003.1312, arXiv:2112.10731] featuring a normal SB0/a-like inner barred spiral disk [arXiv:1005.5294, arXiv:2101.07002] surrounded by an extremely extended outer disk [arXiv:0707.2377, arXiv:astro-ph/0701018]. This massive, gas-rich system [arXiv:1005.5294, arXiv:1906.01657] hosts two compact elliptical satellite galaxies [arXiv:2112.10731] and exhibits scattered, low-level star formation activity across its giant disk [arXiv:1610.00918, arXiv:2101.07002].

sources.summary: The paper reinterprets Malin 1 as a normal luminous early-type galaxy with a huge low-surface-brightness outer disk, rather than a purely dark-matter-dominated LSB system.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 has long been considered a prototype giant, dark matter dominated Low Surface Brightness galaxy. Two recent studies, one based on a re-analysis of VLA observations and the other on an archival Hubble I-band image, throw a new light on this enigmatic galaxy and on its dark/luminous matter properties. Malin 1 is a highly unusual disk galaxy characterized by an enormous, rich and extremely low surface brightness disk . Recent, deep R-band data by Moore Parker show an exponential disk extending out to 124 kpc h_ [ 75 ] ^ 1 (scale length 53 kpc).

Clearly, in its inner luminous part, Malin 1 has the characteristics of an early-type HSB galaxy. Barth has recently published a study of Malin 1 based on archival Hubble I-band data. He has examined the structure and the properties of the inner bright parts and has concluded that Malin 1 has a normal stellar disk and that, out to a radius of 10 kpc, its structure is that of a typical SB0/a galaxy. The new analysis and the optical study throw new light on Malin 1 and on its dark/luminous matter properties.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes panchromatic imaging of Malin 1, finding its giant low-surface-brightness disk can be explained by long, quiet, low-level star formation without a major collision.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 is an iconic giant LSBG --- perhaps the largest disk galaxy known. We attempt to bring new insights on its structure and evolution on the basis of new images covering a wide range in wavelength. We have computed surface brightness profiles (and average surface brightnesses in 16 regions of interest), in six photometric bands ( FUV , NUV , u , g , i , z ).

We find that the surface brightness and color profiles can be reproduced by a long and quiet star-formation history due to the low surface density; no significant event, such as a collision, is necessary. Such quiet star formation across the giant disk is obtained in a disk model calibrated for the Milky Way, but with an angular momentum approximately 20 times larger. Signs of small variations of the star-formation history are indicated by the diversity of ages found when different regions within the galaxy are intercompared. For the first time, panchromatic images of Malin 1 are used to constrain the stellar populations and the history of this iconic example among giant LSBGs. Based on our model, the extreme disk of Malin 1 is found to have a long history of relatively low star formation (about 2 ).

sources.summary: The paper compares an IllustrisTNG analogue to Malin 1, explaining its huge low-surface-brightness disk through merger-triggered cooling of hot halo gas.

quotes.quote: The galaxy Malin 1 contains the largest stellar disk known but the formation mechanism of this structure has been elusive. In this paper, we report a Malin 1 analogue in the 100 Mpc IllustrisTNG simulation and describe its formation history. At redshift zero, this massive galaxy, having a maximum circular velocity V_ max of 430 km \ s^ 1 , contains a 100 kpc gas/stellar disk with morphology similar to Malin 1. The simulated galaxy reproduces well many observed features of Malin 1's vast disk, including its stellar ages, metallicities, and gas rotation curve.

We trace the extended disk back in time and find that a large fraction of the cold gas at redshift zero originated from the cooling of hot halo gas, triggered by the merger of a pair of intruding galaxies. Our finding provides a novel way to form large galaxy disks as extreme as Malin 1 within the current galaxy formation framework.

Recent observations by show spectacular spiral structure made of relatively young ( 0.1 -3 Gyr) stars with metallicities between 0.1-1 Z_ with no apparent stellar age gradient across the disk. A more surprising feature is the large mass with V_ max above 400 \ km \ s^ 1 if the (poorly constrained) inclination is low consistent with, e.g., Galaz2015 . It remains a great challenge to understand the physical process behind a disk that is at least five times larger than the Milky Way.

sources.summary: Malin 1 is modeled with thick stellar and very extended gaseous discs; its rotation curve can fit DM/MOND-like profiles, but inclination and thickness remain uncertain.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 The surface density model adopted here is composed of a thick stellar disc with an exponential profile _s() ( _ cs =6 \ 10 ^3 \ M_ \ pc ^ 2 , r_ ds =2 \ kpc ) and a very extended thin gaseous disc with an exponential profile _g() ( _ cg =4 \ M_ \ pc ^ 2 , r_ dg =30 \ kpc ) (Fig. 8). With these values, the mass of Malin 1 is on the order of 1.7 \ 10 ^ 11 \ M_ . \ Then, a good fit, similar to the predictions of DM or MOND, is obtained for _g= _s= _ but with a global change in the inclination angle from i= 38^ to i= 34^ . However, because the results vary with thickness in the model, a very similar fit is obtained with 2 _g= _s= _ at constant without a change in the inclination i = 38^ . The rotation curves are displayed in Fig. 9.

The DM and the model profiles are very similar. The MOND profile appears slightly less consistent in the outer regions than the two other profiles, but with a change in the inclination angle in the outer parts of only 6^ from i = 38^ to i = 32^ , which cannot be ruled out. According to Lelli et al (2010), MOND is better than both the DM model and the model.

sources.summary: The paper uses MUSE spectroscopy to study Malin 1, finding extended star-forming emission to 100 kpc, measurable dust attenuation, and distinct inner and outer metallicity gradients.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 is the largest GLSB galaxy known to date, and one of the largest individual spiral galaxies observed so far, but the properties and formation mechanisms of its giant disk are still poorly understood. We use VLT/MUSE IFU spectroscopic observations of Malin 1 to measure the star formation rate, dust attenuation, and gas metallicity within this intriguing galaxy. We performed a pPXF modeling to extract emission line fluxes such as , , and along the central region as well as the extended disk of Malin 1. Our observations reveal, for the first time, the presence of strong emission distributed across numerous regions along the extended disk of Malin 1, reaching up to radial distances of 100 kpc, indicating recent star formation activity. We made an estimate of the dust attenuation in the disk of Malin 1 using the Balmer decrement and found that Malin 1 has a mean attenuation of 0.36 mag.

We estimated the gas phase metallicity in Malin 1, and also found for the first time, that the metallicity shows a steep gradient in the inner 20 kpc of the galaxy from solar metallicity to sub-solar values, followed by a flattening of the metallicity in the extended disk with a relatively high value of 0.6 Z_ . We found that the normalized abundance gradient of the inner disk of Malin 1 is similar to values found in normal galaxies. However, the normalized gradient observed in the outer disk can be considered extreme when compared to other disk galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper resolves Malin 1's central low-surface-brightness galaxy core, finding two compact sources whose kinematics and emission lines suggest asymmetric star formation or infalling material.

quotes.quote: The central region of the Giant Low Surface Brightness galaxy Malin 1 has long been known to have a complex morphology with evidence of a bulge, disc, and potentially a bar hosting asymmetric star formation. In this work, we use VLT/MUSE data to resolve the central region of Malin 1 in order to determine its structure. We use careful light profile fitting in every image slice of the datacube to create wavelength-dependent models of each morphological component, from which we could cleanly extract their spectra.

We report the detection of a pair of distinct sources at the centre of this galaxy with a separation of 1.05 , which corresponds to a separation on sky of 1.9 kpc. The radial velocity data of each object confirms that they both lie in the kinematic core of the galaxy, and analysis of the emission lines reveals that the central compact source is more consistent with being ionized by star formation and/or a LINER, while the off-centre compact source lies closer to the separation between star-forming galaxies and AGN. This evidence suggests that the centre of Malin 1 hosts either a bar with asymmetric star formation or two distinct components in which the off-centre compact source could either be a star-forming clump containing one or more star clusters that is in the process of falling into the core of the galaxy and which will eventually merge with the central nuclear star cluster, or a clump of gas infalling into the centre of the galaxy from either outside or from the disc and triggering star formation there.

Since its discovery Bothun_1987 , Malin 1 has always been an unusual member of the class of Giant Low Surface-Brightness (GLSB) galaxies. Not only has it a very faint disk with a surface brightness of 28 mag arcsec ^ 2 in the B band and an extraordinary size of 200 kpc Moore_2006, Galaz_2015, Boissier_2016 , making it perhaps the largest single spiral observed to date, but also it has perplexing features, among them, a large reservoir of 6 10^ 10 M _ of HI pickering_1997 , and to date no direct evidence of molecular gas braine2000, das_2010, galaz2022 .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Malin 1 as a prototypical giant low-surface-brightness galaxy, finding a steep inner rotation curve, baryon-dominated central dynamics, double structure, and tension with MOND.

quotes.quote: We present here a new study of two prototypical GLSB galaxies: Malin 1 and NGC 7589. We re-analysed existing observations and derived new rotation curves, which were used to investigate the distributions of luminous and dark matter in these galaxies. In contrast to previous findings, the rotation curves of both galaxies show a steep rise in the central parts, typical of high surface brightness (HSB) systems.

Mass decompositions with a dark matter halo show that baryons may dominate the dynamics of the inner regions. Indeed, a ``maximum disk'' fit gives stellar mass-to-light ratios in the range of values typically found for HSB galaxies. These results, together with other recent studies, suggest that GLSB galaxies are systems with a double structure: an inner HSB early-type spiral galaxy and an outer extended LSB disk. We also tested the predictions of MOND: the rotation curve of NGC 7589 is reproduced well, whereas Malin 1 represents a challenging test for the theory.

sources.summary: The paper describes Malin 1 as a prototypical giant LSB galaxy with an enormous faint disk and gas-rich warped H I disk, embedded with a barred inner spiral structure.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 is one of the most unusual galaxies known to date. The galaxy was accidentally discovered in the course of a systematic survey of the Virgo cluster region designed to detect extremely low-surface brightness (LSB) galaxies (Bothun et al. 1987). According to observational characteristics, Malin 1 remains unique among other known galaxies. Radial extent of its stellar disc in the V passband reaches out 60 or 90 kpc (adopting distance D_L =366 Mpc and scale 1.51 kpc/1 from the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED)) and the disc scale length is about 45 or 68 kpc (Bothun et al. 1987).

Malin 1 is among the most gas-rich galaxies known to date: M(HI) 7 10^ 10 M _ (Pickering et al. 1997) or 5 10^ 10 M _ (Matthews, van Driel Monnier-Ragaigne 2001). The HI disc of the galaxy demonstrates strong non-circular motions and is strongly warped (Pickering et al. 1997; Lelli, Fraternali Sancisi 2010). Recent analysis of a Hubble Space Telescope I band image suggests that Malin 1 has a normal barred inner spiral disc embedded in a huge diffuse LSB envelope (Barth 2007).

sources.summary: The paper presents deep Magellan images of Malin 1 that reveal faint spiral structure, possible interaction-related features, stellar associations or molecular-gas clumps, and a 160 kpc diameter.

quotes.quote: We present Megacam deep optical images ( g and ) of Malin 1 obtained with the 6.5m Magellan/Clay telescope, detecting structures down to 28 B mag arcsec ^ 2 . In order to enhance galaxy features buried in the noise, we use a noise reduction filter based on the total generalized variation regularizator. This method allows us to detect and resolve very faint morphological features, including spiral arms, with a high visual contrast. For the first time, we can appreciate an optical image of Malin 1 and its morphology in full view.

The analysis suggests that the first one is possibly a background galaxy, and the second is an apparent stream without a clear nature, but could be related to the claimed past interaction between Malin 1 and the galaxy SDSSJ123708.91 + 142253.2. Malin 1 exhibits features suggesting the presence of stellar associations, and clumps of molecular gas, not seen before with such a clarity. Using these images, we obtain a diameter for Malin 1 of 160 kpc, 50 kpc larger than previous estimates.

sources.summary: The paper presents deeper R-band imaging of Malin 1, refining its outer disk extent, inclination, spiral structure, and possible quiescent-merger interpretation.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 is a highly unusual galaxy that remains the most extreme in the class of low surface brightness (LSB) giants Pick . A recent study by suggests the galaxy has an inner normal barred spiral disk (SB0/a) surrounded by a large diffuse LSB envelope. Nevertheless our data show this galaxy's outer disk extends to around 120 kpc making it the largest diffuse outer region of any known disk galaxy. It has a large M _ HI /L _B ratio Imp and appears to be an isolated galaxy that has evolved slowly.

Our data show that the R band optical light from Malin 1 continues even further than the HI mapped by Pick and reproduced by Braine, Herpin, Radford (2000). Contours at 6380 data counts on the image are displayed in Figure contour6380 and trace the irregular outer extent of the galaxy. We also find the radial profile (Figure profile ) extends to almost 80 arcsec, well beyond the V band profiles of Both87 and Pick .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Malin 1 as the largest known disk galaxy and a massive low-surface-brightness benchmark included in Spitzer observations and possible collision-formation scenarios.

quotes.quote: LSB galaxies also include Malin 1 -- the largest disk galaxy known to date. While none of these results contradict the idea that the average LSB galaxy is less evolved than the average HSB galaxy, they do show that we have not yet come close to fully sampling the LSB galaxy parameter space. In addition, it should be emphasized that there may still be large numbers of LSB galaxies with properties beyond our present detection limits.

Recently hinz07 obtained Spitzer observations of five low surface brightness galaxies, two of which are massive LSB systems (Figure fig:dust - UGC 06614 and Malin 1 are the massive LSB galaxies which were studied). Stellar emissions, hot dust, and aromatic molecules were detected from all observed galaxies with uncorrupted data (the 24 m data from one galaxy was unusable).
This theory can clearly explain a number of the massive LSB systems we have seen. such as UGC 06614 and possibly Malin 1.

sources.summary: The paper studies Malin 1's hybrid structure, showing a normal SB0/a-like central region inside a giant low-surface-brightness disk with UV clumps, bar emission, and recent star formation.

quotes.quote: The HST/F814W observation has shown that the central region of Malin 1 is more like a normal SB0/a galaxy, while the rest of the disk has the characteristic of a low surface brightness system. The AstroSat/UVIT observations suggest scattered recent star formation activity all over the disk, especially along the spiral arms. The central 9" ( 14 kpc) region, similar to the size of the Milky Way's stellar disk, has a number of far-UV clumps - indicating recent star-formation activity. The high resolution UVIT/F154W image reveals far-UV emission within the bar region ( 4 kpc) - suggesting the presence of hot, young stars in the bar.

Malin 1 belongs to the class of giant low-surface brightness (GLSB) galaxies and probably, is one of the largest disk galaxies with spiral arms extending upto 130 kpc in radius at the distance of Malin 1 MooreParker06, Boissier2016 . Most of the stars in the outer disk of Malin 1 follows an exponentially declining surface brightness profile.

sources.summary: The BlueMUSE overview treats Malin 1 as a benchmark giant low-surface-brightness galaxy whose huge disk, gas content, star formation, and dynamics motivate large-IFU observations.

quotes.quote: Among LSBs, the galaxy Malin 1, discovered in 1986 1987ApJ...313..629B , is one of the prime examples of giant LSB galaxies, characterized by its extended low surface brightness disk and high gas content. Malin 1 has the largest radial extent of any known spiral galaxy, with a low surface brightness disk extending out to 120 kpc 2006PASA...23..165M and an extrapolated central surface brightness of 0,V 25.5 mag arcsec ^ 2 1997ARA&A..35..267I . Its study will provide a reference point for the family of LSBs with similar \ masses and UV colours. Deep UV and optical imaging showed that the giant disk behaves on long time-scales like a normal galaxy but with an angular momentum as large as 20 the Milky Way one 2015ApJ...815L..29G,2016A&A...593A.126B . This new photometry suggests a variety of ages for the UV emitting regions; a stochastic star formation history for LSBs was also proposed (e.g., 2008ApJ...681..244B ), but this analysis is limited to the GALEX resolution of 5 \ and to the timescales probed by UV filters ( >100 Myr).

sources.summary: The paper treats Malin 1 as a giant low surface brightness galaxy whose halo scale does not explain its giant disc; its two-disc structure favors gas accretion, while a major-merger scenario remains possible.

quotes.quote: Malin 1 . We can rule out the scenario in which the giant size of the disc is due to the peculiarly high radial scale of the dark matter halo, since it contradicts our estimates, Lelli2010 also give moderate value of the radial scale of the dark halo for Malin 1. The presence of a complex structure with two discs makes the scenario of accretion of the gas from the filament on the preformed early-type galaxy more possible for this system. LSB disc could be the result of the accretion. Zhu2018MNRAS proposed a major merging scenario for this galaxy which also does not contradict its observed properties including the absence of the gradient of the stellar age in its disc. However, to make firm conclusion one needs to estimate the stellar velocity dispersion in the region of LSB disc of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper says earlier Malin-1 H I fluxes were likely overestimated, its new flux is somewhat below the VLA value but spectrally consistent, and prior CO searches found no detection.

quotes.quote: Malin-1 (= PGC 42102) : One published Arecibo observation of Malin-1 (see Table 5) gives an integrated \ line flux (2.5 ; Bothun et al. 1987) comparable to the VLA value derived by Pickering et al. (1997), while a re-reduction of the same Arecibo data yielded S_ HI =3.5 Jy \ (Impey Bothun 1989); finally Impey Bothun (1989) measure S_ HI =4.6 \ from a Green Bank 43m spectrum. Our new data indicate that the Arecibo and Green Bank fluxes previously reported by Impey Bothun (1989) are likely to be significantly overestimated. In contrast our new integrated flux (1.80 ) is somewhat lower than the VLA measurement of Pickering et al. (1997), although our values marginally agree to within errors, since at the redshifted frequency of Malin-1 ( 1312 MHz), our absolute calibration uncertainties are expected to be as high as 25 (see Section cal ). In terms of spectral morphology, our new global spectrum shows excellent agreement with that derived by Pickering et al. No CO(1-0) line emission was detected in the central regions of Malin-1 by Radford (1992), with an estimated upper limit of 4.7 10^9 \ to the H _2 mass, and Braine et al. (2000) recently reported an upper limit of M_ H2 /M_ HI <0.03 .

sources.summary: The paper reinterprets Malin 1 as having a normal inner stellar disk plus a distinct, extremely extended low-surface-brightness outer component reaching about 100 kpc.

quotes.quote: Since its discovery, Malin 1 has been considered the prototype and most extreme example of the class of giant low surface brightness disk galaxies. Examination of an archival Hubble Space Telescope I band image reveals that Malin 1 contains a normal stellar disk that was not previously recognized, having a central I band surface brightness of _0 = 20.1 mag arcsec \ and a scale length of 4.8 kpc. Out to a radius of 10 kpc, the structure of Malin 1 is that of a typical SB0/a galaxy. The remarkably extended, faint outer structure detected out to 100 kpc appears to be a photometrically distinct component and not a simple extension of the inner disk.

The presence of a disk of normal size and surface brightness in Malin 1 suggests that such extreme outliers in disk properties probably do not exist, but underscores the importance of the extended outer disk regions for a full understanding of the structure and formation of spiral galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats Malin 1 as the prototype giant low surface brightness galaxy, comparing simulations to its unusually large flat disk and low observed star formation rate.

quotes.quote: Giant low surface brightness galaxies (GLSBs), such as Malin 1, have unusually large and flat discs. Their formation is a puzzle for cosmological simulations in the cold dark matter scenario.
Their prototype is Malin 1 (Bothun et al. 1987).
For example, the star formation rate inferred from observations for Malin 1 is 0.1 M_ yr ^ 1 , whereas the simulated star formation rate is 0.3 M_ yr ^ 1 (Impey Bothun 1989).

sources.summary: The paper treats Malin 1 as the prototype gLSBG, reporting two compact elliptical satellites in HST images, confirmed structurally and spectroscopically, as evidence for merger-linked gLSBG formation.

quotes.quote: Only a couple of dozens gLSBGs were found since the discovery of the prototypical galaxy Malin 1 in the 80s by so they are considered extremely rare.

The inspection of the archival HST images of the class prototype Malin 1 allowed us to make an important step towards understanding of the nature of gLSBGs. Malin 1 appeared to have two satellites which reminded the low-mass dense compact elliptical galaxies (cE) (see Fig. ce ). The cEs are rare low-mass systems ( 0.5 of dwarf galaxy population) with small sizes ( r_e<1 kpc) and high densities typically hosting old metal-rich stars . They likely originate from tidal stripping of massive disky progenitors losing 90--95 of stellar mass . The structural analysis of the Hubble WFPC2 data using GALFIT confirmed the cE classification, and the re-analysis of published optical spectra from the Russian 6-m telescope confirmed that these cE were indeed the satellites of Malin 1.

sources.summary: The paper describes Malin 1 as a very massive low surface brightness disk galaxy, grouped with DDO 154 as a high mass-to-light object posing a suppressed star-formation problem.

quotes.quote: The first such examples were dwarf irregular galaxies with extended \ envelopes; DDO 154 serves as the archetype of this class of gas-rich LSB dwarfs KrummBurstein . At the other end of the mass scale, the serendipitous discovery of Malin 1 BothunEtAl, ImpeyBothun , a very massive, dominated LSB disk galaxy, provided an example of the class of ``crouching giants'' predicted by Disney . A common feature of Malin 1 and DDO 154 is their high \ mass:light ratios: M / L_B 5 [ compared to M / L_B 0.1 --1 for `normal' detected LSB galaxies FisherTully, Waugh ] . In addition to the observational problem of determining their abundance --- which PASS has begun to address --- galaxies like DDO 154 and Malin 1 pose the following theoretical problem: ``Why was there so little gas processed into stars'' in these systems CarignanBeaulieu ? Are these objects exceptional in the way in which they are presently forming stars?

sources.summary: The paper cites Malin 1 as an isolated spiral with an extremely extended low-surface-brightness gas disk and a flat HI rotation curve reaching 100 kpc.

quotes.quote: A remarkable example is Malin 1, which is a spiral galaxy exhibiting an extremely large low surface brightness disk of gas that is five times wider than the Milky Way (figure 19). Thus, Lelli, Fraternali, and Sancisi (2010) provide a flat rotation curve up to 100 \ kpc for Malin 1 using HI data. However, Malin 1 is a relatively isolated galaxy, and it is very likely that the flat profile does not continue forever for any galaxy, especially when in the outermost regions of the disk, interaction with the environment, i.e., the influence of a neighboring galaxy, begins to be detected.

gmw_00000565.name: GN20
gmw_00000565.overview: GN20 is a gas-rich, hyper-luminous submillimeter galaxy at redshift 4.05 [arXiv:1209.2418, arXiv:2302.10323] that contains a clumpy, rotating molecular gas disk [arXiv:1209.2418, arXiv:1112.3480]. It is the brightest member of a proto-cluster [arXiv:2304.13529] and exhibits vigorous starburst activity [arXiv:1109.1140, arXiv:2304.13529] likely triggered by gravitational interactions [arXiv:2304.13529, arXiv:2402.18672].

sources.summary: The paper presents GN20 as a securely CO-detected z=4.055 GOODS-N SMG, the field's brightest submillimeter source, with long-wavelength dust continuum and an infrared luminosity implying star formation above 1000 solar masses per year.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The B configuration observations within the same velocity range results in a detection with S/N=13 and a position of (J2000)= 12^h37^m11^s.90 , (J2000)= 62^d22^m12^s.1 (Fig. fig:2Da left).

quotes.quote: GN20 is very securely detected in both D- and B-configuration observations (Fig. fig:2Da left). Most of the signal appears to be due to a CO emission line. Averaging the signal from 150 km s ^ 1 to 900 km s ^ 1 (here and in the following all velocities are defined with respect to the central frequency of the observations at 91.375 GHz) results in a detection with S/N=10 for the D-configuration data (Fig. fig:2Da left and Fig. fig:1D top). The B configuration observations within the same velocity range results in a detection with S/N=13 and a position of (J2000)= 12^h37^m11^s.90 , (J2000)= 62^d22^m12^s.1 (Fig. fig:2Da left).

This analysis implies that GN20 and GN20.2 are extremely luminous galaxies, with L_ IR >10^ 13 L_ . Besides being among the most distant known SMGs, these are also some of the most luminous galaxies known so far. We use a Kennicutt et al. (1998) conversion of SFR [ M _ yr ^ 1 ] = L_ IR [ L_ ] /10^ 10 , expressed for the Chabrier 2003 IMF adopted in this paper. The IR luminosities correspond to SFRs of >1000M_ yr ^ 1 if the IR emission is dominated by star formation.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA CO observations of GN20, finding a z=4.05 submillimeter galaxy with a clumpy 14 kpc rotating gas disk, massive molecular reservoir, and extended star formation.

quotes.quote: Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) observations of the CO(2--1) emission in the z=4.05 submillimeter galaxy (SMG) GN20. These high--resolution data allow us to image the molecular gas at 1.3 kpc resolution just 1.6 Gyr after the Big Bang. The data reveal a clumpy, extended gas reservoir, 14 4 kpc in diameter, in unprecedented detail. A dynamical analysis shows that the data are consistent with a rotating disk of total dynamical mass 5.4 2.4 10 ^ 11 M _ .

We identify five distinct molecular gas clumps in the disk of GN20 with masses a few percent of the total gas mass, brightness temperatures of 16--31K, and surface densities of >3,200-4,500 ( _ CO /0.8 ) M _ pc ^ 2 . Virial mass estimates indicate they could be self--gravitating, and we constrain their CO--to--H _2 mass conversion factor to be < 0.2--0.7 M _ (K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 )^ 1 . A multiwavelength comparison demonstrates that the molecular gas is concentrated in a region of the galaxy that is heavily obscured in the rest--frame UV/optical.
From the dynamical modeling, we derive a dynamical mass for GN20 of 5.4 2.4 10 ^ 11 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper describes GN20 as a bright z=4.055 submillimeter galaxy in a proto-cluster, with CO detections, vigorous star formation, a clumpy extended gas disk, and double-peaked CO(4-3) emission.

quotes.quote: GN20 is one of the brightest SMGs in the GOODS-N field pope06 , of which the redshift ( z=4.055 ) was established by a serendipitous detection of its CO(4-3) emission daddi09b .

All three massive SMGs in the GN20 proto-cluster have been detected in CO emission, indicative of large amounts of molecular gas daddi09b,carilli10,carilli11,hodge12,hodge13 , feeding vigorous ongoing star formation (SFR a few to ten times 100 M_ yr ^ 1 ). The deep, high-resolution CO(2-1) observations reveal a clumpy, extended gas disk (14 4 kpc in diameter) for GN20 hodge12 , and extended gas reservoirs ( 5--8 kpc) for GN20.2a and GN20.2b hodge13 . hodge12,hodge13 have attempted to constrain the estimate of CO-to-H _2 conversion factor by dynamical analysis, deriving _ CO of 1--2 for these three galaxies. For the dust continuum emission, however, only GN20 has been reported in Herschel and (sub)mm detections daddi09b,magdis11 .

To measure the velocity centroid and line width of CO emission of GN20, we fitted Gaussians to the observed spectra (Fig. fig1 ), which is extracted at the fixed CO position from daddi09b , allowing for the presence of faint underlying continuum. The CO(4-3) spectrum of GN20 shows clear evidence of double-peaked structure. A single Gaussian fit to the spectrum shown in Fig. fig1 yields a peak flux density of 2.13 0.14 mJy and an FWHM of 630 51 , consistent with daddi09b .
