On the basis of the above description regarding the tidal tails and bridges, the kinematics and the star formation, we argue that Galaxy 1 is likely to be the retrograde interacting companion of Galaxy 4. This view agrees with the results of evolutionary synthesis modelling of the galaxies tf03 , which indicate an age of 180 Myr for the interaction-induced bursts in both galaxies, consistent with estimated age of the strong tidal tail. In this case the cone-like plume could have originated from Galaxy 1. Assuming that the R-band luminosity of the two galaxies roughly traces their masses, this would be a minor merger, with an approximate mass ratio 4:1 for Galaxy 4: Galaxy 1.

sources.summary: The paper describes Galaxy 1 in CG J1720-67.8 as a blue starburst, likely stripped Sc-type galaxy connected to Galaxy 2, with a distorted velocity pattern.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 1: A blue (B V = 0.47) starburst galaxy with an exponential bulge and a bulge-to-total light ratio B/T 0.4, whose general properties are consistent with an Sc type. Since no spiral arms are visible, the galaxy could have lost its outermost layers in the interaction. A bridge of matter apparently connects this galaxy to galaxy 2.
Galaxy 1 shows a distorted velocity pattern, although the observed velocity gradient across the galaxy is very small ( 50 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper models G1 as a late-type interacting spiral whose observed spectra and SED favor a strong, older interaction-induced burst of star formation.

quotes.quote: In fact, the high bulge to total light ratio B/T > 0.4 of G1 could be a consequence of the central star formation, which might mimic the presence of an important bulge in an otherwise late-type-looking galaxy.

In case of both moderate and strong bursts (Figs. g1CC1spec a) and b) ), the models which better approach the observed spectra of G1 are those at a burst age in the range 40 - 180 Myr, with the oldest age favored by the calculated reduced- ^2 . ^ a Observed colors after the application of a standard inclination correction. ^ a Observed luminosities after the application of a standard inclination correction.

We found that the two late-type group's spirals, G1 and G4, have SEDs and optical spectra in reasonable agreement with a CC-model of an Sc galaxy at an age 40 -- 180 Myr after the onset of an interaction-induced intermediate to strong burst of star formation. In particular the strong and older burst offers the preferred solution for G1, while intermediate ages and strengths between the two proposed models might be more suitable to G4.

sources.summary: The paper treats G1 as a smaller interacting spiral in CG J1720-67.8, with a recent starburst, measured model mass, shared velocity with G4, and bridge-linked encounter modeling.

quotes.quote: The total (stars + gas) masses of the galaxies implied by the best-fit models are 3.4 -- 7 10 ^ 10 M _ (depending on the considered model) for G2, 4 -- 6 10 ^ 9 M _ for G1, and 2 10 ^ 10 M _ for G4.

Since the two spiral galaxies G1 and G4 have similar systemic velocities and appear to be connected by a bridge of ionized gas, we first compared the observations with an encounter between two unequal mass disks. The best-fit evolutionary synthesis models imply for these two galaxies a mass ratio 1:3 or 1:4, hence a 1:3 mass ratio was adopted in the simulations.
Galaxies G1 and G4, and the bridge of ionized gas between them have similar systemic radial velocities.

gmw_00000577.name: Galaxy 2
gmw_00000577.overview: Galaxy 2 is a reddened lenticular galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0603508, arXiv:astro-ph/0509905] dominated by a de Vaucouleurs bulge [arXiv:astro-ph/0410014, arXiv:astro-ph/0301108]. It exhibits active star formation in its central region [arXiv:astro-ph/0410014, arXiv:astro-ph/0509905] and may be the remnant of a merger that occurred approximately 1 Gyr ago [arXiv:astro-ph/0603508, arXiv:astro-ph/0301108].

sources.summary: The paper finds Galaxy 2 has limited ionized-gas kinematic coverage, central star formation, and may be a merger remnant linked to faint tails and plumes.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, the highest values of velocity dispersion are found in the central/southern part of Galaxy 2 and in the northern part of Galaxy 4, where the two galaxies partly overlap. The broadening of the emission-line in this region might indicate the presence of an unresolved second component at slightly different radial velocity, originating in Galaxy 2.
Galaxy 2 shows some velocity gradient, but the ionized gas was detected in too small a region to allow a fitting of the velocity field and the extraction of a velocity curve.

Since Galaxy 2 has a de Vaucouleurs bulge and a weak disc component and exhibits active star formation in its central parts (although at low level), we suggest the possibility that Galaxy 2 is the remnant of a relatively recent merger. If this were the case, the central star formation could be interpreted as the remains of a burst triggered during the merging phase. The faint tail and plumes could be the tidal debris of such a merger, most probably involving two relatively gas-poor galaxies (e.g. two early-type spirals).

sources.summary: The paper identifies G2 as the S0 member of CG J1720-67.8, assigns it the largest model mass, links it to an older burst or merger, and includes it in interaction simulations.

quotes.quote: The group CG J1720-67.8 , composed of two spiral galaxies (G1 and G4), one S0 galaxy (G2), and a number of tidal dwarf galaxy candidates in a very compact configuration (Fig. temp:fig1 ), appears to be next to the final merging .

An older burst of star formation in the S0 galaxy accompanied either a merger event or an interaction with the companions, 1 Gyr ago. The total (stars + gas) masses of the galaxies implied by the best-fit models are 3.4 -- 7 10 ^ 10 M _ (depending on the considered model) for G2, 4 -- 6 10 ^ 9 M _ for G1, and 2 10 ^ 10 M _ for G4.

To test this hypothesis, we introduced the S0 galaxy in our simulation. The formation of the S0 galaxy was simulated through an equal mass disk merger. After 1 Gyr a massive disk approaches the S0 and starts a burst of star formation accompanied by galactic winds (Fig. temp:fig2 , left).

sources.summary: The paper describes Galaxy 2 as an S0 system with a dominant bulge, central star-formation traces, possible merger origin, and counter-rotation.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 2: An S0 galaxy (B V = 0.88) with a de Vaucouleurs bulge, which dominates its light (B/T 0.7), and traces of central star formation. Evolutionary synthesis models suggest that this galaxy might be the result of a 1 Gyr old merger.
Galaxy 2, with a velocity gradient of 120 km s ^ 1 in NE-SW direction, shows a counter-rotation with respect to galaxy 1.

sources.summary: G2 is described as a reddened early-type/S0 member with present-day central star formation; the paper weighs merger and interaction-triggered burst scenarios for its observed properties.

quotes.quote: All the group's members show blue colors, with the exception of G2 whose observed red color ( B-V = 0.98) is probably a combined effect of the high internal reddening (A _V = 1.6 mag, as derived from the Balmer decrement in its spectrum) and of its metallicity (Z 0.5 Z _ , based on emission line measurements), higher than that of all other group's members, which have Z 0.3 Z _ or lower (see Papers II and III).
Both photometric and spectroscopic results (Paper II) have shown that G2 is a strongly reddened early-type galaxy with evidence of present-day star formation activity at its center.

7.1) we have proposed two possible explanations for the observed properties of G2: either this galaxy is the outcome of a merger that induced a (now fading) burst of star formation and produced part of the faint tails and plumes visible in the group, or it is an early-type galaxy which started a small episode of star formation as a consequence of tidal interaction with the companion spiral galaxies.
